node:uv-coordinates.title: UV Coords
node:uv-coordinates.tagline: Gives you the position of each pixel as a vec2 (0–1), so you can drive patterns and effects from screen position.
node:uv-coordinates.description: This node outputs the current position on the screen as a vec2: two numbers (x, y) that go from 0 to 1 across the image. UV coordinates are the standard way to feed "where am I on the screen?" into pattern nodes like Noise, Translate, or Scale. Bottom-left is (0, 0), top-right is (1, 1).

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Position on screen (0–1 in x and y). Connect to Noise, Translate, Scale, or any node that takes vec2 coordinates.

examples: Connect to Noise, then Output to see a grayscale pattern (floats promote to vec3).
Feed into Translate or Scale to shift or zoom patterns.
Use with Polar Coords or Hex Grid for radial or tiled effects.
node:uv-coordinates.advanced: UV is normalized (0–1). Use Fragment Coords when you need pixel coordinates or aspect-ratio corrections; use Resolution with Divide for that.

node:time.title: Time
node:time.tagline: A number that keeps increasing over time—use it to animate motion, waves, or any value that should change as the shader runs.
node:time.description: Time outputs a single number (a float) that increases every frame. You can multiply it to speed up or slow down animations, or feed it into Sine/Cosine for smooth waves. It has no inputs and no parameters—it always gives you the current time.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Current time in seconds. Connect to Sine, Cosine, Multiply, or Noise to drive animation.

examples: Connect to Sine or Cosine, then Output for a moving wave (floats promote to vec3).
Multiply Time by a small float to slow down (or large to speed up) animation.
Feed into Noise Time input for animated noise, or into Translate for moving patterns.

node:mixed-wave-signal.title: Mixed Wave
node:mixed-wave-signal.tagline: A single float signal built from three weighted sine or cosine waves—good for organic, controllable motion without full noise.
node:mixed-wave-signal.description: Uses shader time internally (you do not need a separate Time node). Global speed and offset scale the phase for all waves. Each wave has its own speed, phase offset, weight, and whether it uses sine or cosine (0 = sine, 1 = cosine). The three contributions are blended by weight and normalized so the mix stays in the −1 to 1 range, then mapped linearly to Out min and Out max (both between −1 and 1). Connect the output to parameters (via automation), math nodes, or Output for grayscale preview.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Remapped signal. Typical range follows Out min / Out max; use with Multiply, Mix, or drive animated parameters.

parameters.name: globalSpeed
parameters.description: Multiplies time before per-wave speeds; negative plays backward.

parameters.name: globalOffset
parameters.description: Adds a constant phase in radians to all waves (same as shifting all waves together).

parameters.name: w0Speed, w1Speed, w2Speed
parameters.description: Per-wave frequency relative to the global-scaled time.

parameters.name: w0Offset, w1Offset, w2Offset
parameters.description: Phase offset in radians for each wave (detunes beating between waves).

parameters.name: w0Weight, w1Weight, w2Weight
parameters.description: Relative mix; the three waves are averaged with these weights so the result stays bounded.

parameters.name: w0Shape, w1Shape, w2Shape
parameters.description: Per-wave waveform: Sine, Cosine, Square (sign of sine), Triangle (asin∘sin), Saw up/down (ramp over 2π), Abs sine, or Smooth square (softened edges). Legacy presets with w*Cosine load as Sine or Cosine.

parameters.name: outMin, outMax
parameters.description: Final linear map: −1…1 blend maps to this interval (both ends must stay in −1…1).

examples: Tune Wave 2/3 speeds slightly off 1.0 to get quasi-periodic, non-repeating motion.
Set Cosine to 1 on one wave for a 90° phase shift vs sine-only waves.
Use Out min / Out max to bias motion (e.g. 0 to 0.5) before Multiply into a parameter.

node:oscillator-2d.title: Oscillator 2D
node:oscillator-2d.tagline: Stacked sine motion on X and Y with optional rotation and offset—drive paired float parameters without building the math patch by hand.
node:oscillator-2d.description: Uses shader time (no separate Time node). Layer mix (same for X and Y) chooses how the three partial waves on each axis combine before rotation: Sum (additive); Normalized (sum divided by a small ε plus the sum of absolute amplitudes for enabled layers, which reduces blow-up when many layers are loud); Product (multiplies enabled terms amp×sin; disabled layers count as multiplicative 1, so muting one layer does not zero the stack—if all layers on an axis are off, that axis is 0); Max absolute value (the signed partial at the largest |amp×sin| among enabled layers; ties use the first layer in X1→X3 or Y1→Y3 order). Each partial has an On toggle in the same row as Amp, Freq, and Phase so you can mute a layer without changing its amplitude. Parameters are grouped as X1–X3 and Y1–Y3; each group has On, Amp, Freq, and Phase. Layers can use mismatched frequencies for organic drift. Rotation uses Speed, Phase, and wobble (Wobble, Wob freq, Wob phase). Outputs are rotated in the XY plane, then offset X/Y is added last. On all float knobs and value strips in the app, vertical drag is finer when the value is near zero in absolute terms (easier to land small numbers like 0.05–0.2). Shift still gives extra-fine control. Coordinate scale matches typical distortion sliders (p-style units, roughly O(±1)). Connect X→Center X and Y→Center Y (or any float pair); parameter input modes on the target still behave per axis.

outputs.name: x
outputs.description: Horizontal oscillator value after stacks, rotation and offset.

outputs.name: y
outputs.description: Vertical oscillator value after stacks, rotation and offset.

examples: Wire Oscillator X and Y into Vortex center X/Y; keep small amplitudes and layer three slightly different frequencies.
Set Rotation speed to spin the oscillator vector together; Rot wobble amplitude modulates spin rate subtly.
Use Offset to park the wandering path near a baseline while the stacks move around it.
On the target parameter, multiply mode combines the oscillator with the pad slider for proportional motion.

node:resolution.title: Resolution
node:resolution.tagline: Use this to access screen resolution for aspect ratio calculations and pixel-perfect effects
node:resolution.description: Outputs the screen resolution as a vec2 containing the width and height in pixels. This is essential for maintaining aspect ratios, calculating pixel sizes, and creating resolution-independent effects. The x component represents width, and the y component represents height. Connect Fragment Coords to Divide (a) and Resolution to Divide (b) for aspect-correct UV; or feed Resolution into UV or Polar Coords for pixel-aware effects.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Screen resolution as (width, height) in pixels. Connect to Divide with Fragment Coords for aspect-correct coords, or to Bayer Dither and similar effects.

examples: Divide Fragment Coords by Resolution to get normalized 0–1 coords that respect aspect ratio.
Use with UV or Polar Coords to scale or dither in pixel space.
Feed width/height into Bayer Dither or other pixel-aware effects for consistent look at any resolution.
node:resolution.advanced: Resolution is in pixels (x = width, y = height). For aspect-correct UV, use Fragment Coords divided by Resolution; for simple 0–1 space use UV Coords.

node:fragment-coordinates.title: Frag Coords
node:fragment-coordinates.tagline: Perfect for accessing raw pixel coordinates for pixel-level effects and precise positioning
node:fragment-coordinates.description: Outputs the fragment coordinates (gl_FragCoord.xy) as a vec2, representing the actual pixel position on the screen. Unlike UV coordinates which are normalized, fragment coordinates are in actual pixel units, with (0,0) at the bottom-left corner. This is useful for pixel-perfect effects, precise positioning, and calculations that require actual screen dimensions. Connect to Divide with Resolution for aspect-correct 0–1 coords, or feed into Translate, Bayer Dither, or Polar Coords for pixel-aware effects.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Fragment coordinates in pixel units (x, y). Connect to Divide (a) with Resolution (b) for normalized coords, or to pixel-based effects like Bayer Dither.

examples: Divide by Resolution for aspect-correct 0–1 coordinates; connect to Noise or Translate for pixel-aligned effects.
Use for Bayer Dither or Polar Coords when you need actual pixel position.
Feed into nodes that expect pixel units (e.g. precise positioning or resolution-dependent patterns).
node:fragment-coordinates.advanced: Fragment coords are in pixel space (bottom-left origin). Use when you need real pixel position or aspect ratio; use UV Coords when 0–1 normalized space is enough.

node:orbit-camera.title: Orbit Camera
node:orbit-camera.tagline: Camera orbiting a target — feeds ro/rd into 3D SDF / raymarch nodes
node:orbit-camera.description: Outputs ray origin (ro) and ray direction (rd) from a camera that orbits a target, driven by time. Connect Screen position (e.g. from UV Coords) so each pixel gets the correct ray direction; without it every pixel shoots the same forward ray and the connected SDF collapses to a single dot. Speed can be negative to reverse the orbit, and Phase offsets the start angle so two cameras can orbit the same target out of sync. Connect ro and rd to nodes that expose ro/rd inputs (Box / Torus SDF, SDF Raymarch, Bokeh Point, …) to share this camera.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Screen position for per-pixel ray direction. Should be connected — UV Coords or Fragment Coordinates work; without a connection rays don't vary per pixel and the result is a single dot.

outputs.name: ro
outputs.description: Ray origin (camera position on the orbit)

outputs.name: rd
outputs.description: Ray direction (normalized, per fragment when Screen position is connected)

parameters.name: Radius
parameters.description: Orbit distance from the target — larger values pull the camera back. Combine with Target X/Y/Z to frame the scene.

parameters.name: Speed
parameters.description: Orbit angular speed in radians per second. Negative reverses the spin; 0 freezes the camera (Phase still controls where it sits).

parameters.name: Phase
parameters.description: Start-angle offset in degrees. Use to offset two cameras orbiting the same target, or to park a frozen camera (Speed = 0) at a specific azimuth.

parameters.name: Target X
parameters.description: World X of the look-at target the camera orbits around.

parameters.name: Target Y
parameters.description: World Y of the look-at target.

parameters.name: Target Z
parameters.description: World Z of the look-at target.

parameters.name: Tilt (rad)
parameters.description: Inclination in radians (±π/2 ≈ ±1.57). 0 keeps the camera level on the XZ plane; positive tilts it up over the target. Approaching ±π/2 pushes the camera near the world-up basis and rays can become unstable — back off slightly if you see glitchy directions.

parameters.name: FOV
parameters.description: Screen-spread multiplier applied to the per-pixel offset (1.0 is neutral). Larger widens the field; smaller narrows it. This is the inverse sense of Look-at Camera's Zoom (which is an image-plane distance) — both shape framing, but FOV grows wider as you turn it up while Zoom grows tighter.

examples: Connect UV Coords → in, then ro/rd into an SDF or raymarch node for an orbiting 3D view.
Set Speed to 0 and use Phase to park the camera at a specific azimuth for a still render.
Use a negative Speed (and matching Phase offset) on a second Orbit Camera to make two objects counter-orbit the same target.
node:orbit-camera.advanced: Orbit moves the camera around a target over time; use Look-at Camera when you want a fixed camera position and explicit forward direction. The orbit basis uses world up (0,1,0); near ±π/2 Tilt the camera approaches that axis and the right/up vectors degenerate — keep Tilt a touch under the limit if rays glitch. FOV here is a screen-spread multiplier (higher = wider) and is the inverse sense of Look-at Camera's Zoom (higher = narrower); both shape framing, just with opposite knob direction.

node:look-at-camera.title: Look-at Camera
node:look-at-camera.tagline: Ray origin and direction from a fixed camera looking at a target (no orbit)
node:look-at-camera.description: Outputs ray origin (ro) and ray direction (rd) from a fixed camera position looking at a target. Use for drive-style or fixed-view 3D (e.g. bokeh lights, raymarching). Connect Screen position (e.g. UV Coords, or UV minus rain offset) for perspective. Position is the camera; Look-at target is the point it faces. Zoom moves the imaging plane forward along the view (focal-plane style distance): larger values usually feel more telephoto (narrower spread), smaller values wider; it is not magnify-style optical zoom.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Screen position for per-pixel ray direction (e.g. UV or normalized device coords)

outputs.name: ro
outputs.description: Ray origin (camera position)

outputs.name: rd
outputs.description: Ray direction (normalized, per fragment when Screen position is connected)

parameters.name: Position X
parameters.description: Camera world X. Together with Position Y/Z, sets ray origin before look direction is built.

parameters.name: Position Y
parameters.description: Camera world Y.

parameters.name: Position Z
parameters.description: Camera world Z.

parameters.name: Look-at target X
parameters.description: World X of the point the camera looks toward; forward is normalized from Position to this target.

parameters.name: Look-at target Y
parameters.description: World Y of the look-at point.

parameters.name: Look-at target Z
parameters.description: World Z of the look-at point.

parameters.name: Zoom
parameters.description: Distance along the view axis from the camera to the plane where Screen position spreads into ray offsets. Larger values push that plane farther forward (typically narrower / more telephoto); smaller pulls it closer (wider cone). This is not magnify-style zoom—compare with Orbit Camera’s FOV control.

examples: Connect UV to in; connect ro and rd to an SDF or Bokeh node for 3D raymarching.
Use with Rain Drops: subtract rain UV offset from UV, then feed into Look-at Camera for windshield effect.
Often used with Bokeh Point or Drive Home Lights for drive-style scenes.
node:look-at-camera.advanced: Look-at keeps the camera fixed; use Orbit Camera when you want the camera to move around a target over time. Basis construction uses world up (0,1,0); when the camera’s forward direction is nearly parallel to that axis, rays can become unstable or degenerate—tilt Position or Look-at target slightly if you see glitchy directions. Larger Zoom moves the imaging plane farther along forward (usually a tighter field); combine it with Position and Look-at target for framing rather than expecting magnify-style optical zoom.

node:rain-drops.title: Rain Drops
node:rain-drops.tagline: Procedural rain-on-glass UV offset for windshield distortion
node:rain-drops.description: Outputs a vec2 UV offset for rain drops on glass. Subtract from UV and feed to Look-at Camera for windshield distortion. Parameters: scale, seed, speed, layers, drop size variation, quantity per layer. Reference: Shadertoy The Drive Home.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: UV coordinates

inputs.name: time
inputs.description: Time for animation (default: internal time)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: UV offset to subtract before camera (uv - out → camera)

examples: Feed UV into in and subtract the output from UV before connecting to Look-at Camera for windshield rain distortion.
Connect Time to the time input to animate drops; tune scale and quantity for density.
Use with Drive Home Lights: same UV pipeline (UV − rain offset → camera) for a rainy drive scene.

node:bokeh-point.title: Bokeh Point
node:bokeh-point.tagline: Soft bokeh disc intensity for one 3D point light
node:bokeh-point.description: Outputs 0–1 intensity for a single 3D point as seen from a ray (ro, rd). Size is the world-space band from closest ray–point approach: smaller values need a tighter pass to light up; Blur only softens the outer edge. Multiply the output by light color. Inner falloff optionally darkens the disc center for a less flat core. Reference: Shadertoy The Drive Home.

inputs.name: ro
inputs.description: Ray origin. Connect ro from Look-at Camera or Orbit Camera.

inputs.name: rd
inputs.description: Ray direction. Connect rd from the same camera as ro.

inputs.name: point
inputs.description: Light position in 3D. Connect Constant Vec3 or any vec3 world-space position.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Disc intensity 0–1 (not the Blur parameter). Connect to Multiply with a color, Output, Mix, or toward Final Output.

parameters.name: Size
parameters.description: World-space crossing band: based on the shortest distance from the ray line to the point (perpendicular distance). Larger values light up from farther passes; very small values need an almost direct hit.

parameters.name: Blur
parameters.description: Edge softness of the disc (smoothstep range). Higher values spread the falloff over a wider distance band at the rim; does not change the peak center value.

parameters.name: Inner falloff
parameters.description: When on, slightly darkens the center of the disc relative to the rim for a less flat highlight. When off, the core stays brighter and more uniform.

examples: Connect Look-at or Orbit Camera ro/rd, and a Constant Vec3 as the light position; multiply output by a color and add to your scene.
Use several Bokeh Point nodes with different positions and colors for multiple lights.
Feed the float output into Output or Multiply with a color, then add to Final Output or mix with background.

node:bokeh.title: Bokeh
node:bokeh.tagline: Fullscreen post bokeh: iris highlights (WebGPU multipass before Final Output; inline stub when blended upstream)
node:bokeh.description: Not the same as **Bokeh Point** (3D disc on a ray). This node is a **post effect** on a vec4 color chain: **WebGPU** uses a **multipass** plan when it sits **directly before Final Output** (bright pass, shaped blur, recombine). If you wire it **into another node** (e.g. **Blend Color**), WebGPU uses the same **single-pass approximation** as the WebGL preview so the graph stays on WebGPU. **WebGL** always uses that lightweight luminance + angular iris modulation. Place before **Final Output** for full multipass quality, or upstream in a color chain for a quick effect.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Scene or graded color. Typical chain: … → Bokeh → Final Output.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Combined color with bokeh highlights baked in (alpha preserved where supported).

examples: Chain Output → Bokeh → Final Output when WebGPU builds the multipass iris blur.
Compare with **Bokeh Point** when you want a literal 3D light disc from ro/rd, not a 2D post bloom.
Raise Threshold then Intensity/Strength/Radius until only bright regions bloom; tune Blades + Rotation on WebGPU for aperture shape.

node:drive-home-lights.title: Drive Home Lights
node:drive-home-lights.tagline: Bokeh street, head, tail, and environment lights in one node
node:drive-home-lights.description: Outputs additive light color (not a full lit scene): street lights, headlights, tail and brake lights, and environment lights as soft bokeh discs, plus a sky tint built from two OKLCH colors. How much sky you see still follows ray elevation—looking toward the horizon stays darker; the low/high colors only tint the part of the sky that is already visible. Multiply or add with other colors as needed; combine with Look-at Camera (ro/rd) and Time. Reference: Shadertoy The Drive Home.

inputs.name: ro
inputs.description: Ray origin. Connect from Look-at Camera or Orbit Camera.

inputs.name: rd
inputs.description: Ray direction. Connect from the same camera as ro.

inputs.name: time
inputs.description: Time for animation. Connect Time node for driving light motion.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Additive light color including built-in sky tint (two OKLCH stops + ray falloff) and strength

parameters.name: Sky low L
parameters.description: OKLCH lightness for the sky tint when the ray looks lower—pairs with the left swatch above.

parameters.name: Sky low C
parameters.description: OKLCH chroma for the low-elevation sky tint (left swatch). Higher is more saturated.

parameters.name: Sky low H
parameters.description: OKLCH hue (degrees) for the low-elevation sky tint (left swatch).

parameters.name: Sky high L
parameters.description: OKLCH lightness for the sky tint when the ray looks higher—pairs with the right swatch above.

parameters.name: Sky high C
parameters.description: OKLCH chroma for the high-elevation sky tint (right swatch).

parameters.name: Sky high H
parameters.description: OKLCH hue (degrees) for the high-elevation sky tint (right swatch).

parameters.name: Sky strength
parameters.description: Scales how strong the sky tint contribution is on top of the bokeh lights. Does not replace the horizon darkening—elevation still controls how much sky appears.

parameters.name: Time scale
parameters.description: Multiplies the connected time input before driving all motion (traffic, flicker, loops). Higher scrolls the drive faster.

parameters.name: Tail-light lanes
parameters.description: Biases how tail lights pick a lane side from procedural noise—higher values favor one pattern of left/right spacing; lower values favor the opposite. Adjust for variety, not overall road position.

examples: Connect ro/rd from Look-at Camera and Time; plug output into Final Output or add with a sky gradient.
Combine with Rain Drops: use (UV − rain offset) as the screen input to the same Look-at Camera for a rainy drive.

Use sky low/high to tint the visible sky (defaults match the classic single purple); darkness near the horizon comes from ray elevation, not a separate “black” color stop. Adjust sky strength, time scale, and Tail-light lanes to match the drive mood.

node:constant-float.title: Float
node:constant-float.tagline: A single number you set with a slider. Use it wherever another node needs a float (speed, strength, threshold, etc.).
node:constant-float.description: Float gives you one number that you control with the Value slider. No inputs needed—just add the node and set the value. Connect it to Multiply to scale something, to Mix to set blend amount, to Translate or Scale for offsets, or to any port that expects a float.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The number set by the Value parameter. Connect to Add, Multiply, Mix, Translate, Scale, or any float input.

parameters.name: Value
parameters.description: The constant number this node outputs. Change it to set speed, strength, offset, or any single value you feed downstream (e.g. to Multiply, Mix, or Translate).

examples: Set Value and connect to Multiply to scale noise or animation speed; to Mix for blend amount; to Translate or Scale for offset.
Use as fallback when a float input is unconnected (e.g. Mix amount, Sine frequency).
Connect to Sine or Cosine for a fixed phase or frequency when you don't need Time.

node:constant-vec2.title: Vec2
node:constant-vec2.tagline: Ideal for providing constant 2D vectors for coordinates, offsets, and 2D parameters
node:constant-vec2.description: Outputs a constant vec2 value with adjustable x and y components. This node is perfect for providing fixed 2D values such as coordinate offsets, scale factors, translation amounts, or any pair of related float values. Both x and y components can be independently adjusted between -1000.0 and 1000.0. Connect to Translate or Scale for offsets, to Distance (a or b) for a center point, or to Noise and other nodes that take vec2.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The constant vec2 value with (x, y) components. Connect to Translate, Scale, Distance, or any vec2 input.

examples: Set x, y, z and connect to Translate or Scale for 2D offset/scale; to Rotate for center; to UV-based nodes for fixed vec2.
Use as a constant offset to Add with UV or other vec2 for shifted patterns.
Feed into Noise, Hex Grid, or Wave Patterns as a fixed coordinate or scale input.

node:constant-vec3.title: Vec3
node:constant-vec3.tagline: Perfect for providing constant 3D vectors for positions, directions, or RGB color values
node:constant-vec3.description: Outputs a constant vec3 value with adjustable x, y, and z components. This node is useful for providing fixed 3D values such as positions, direction vectors, RGB color values (before conversion to color nodes), or any triple of related float values. All three components can be independently adjusted between -1000.0 and 1000.0. Connect to Bokeh Point (point), Look-at Camera (position/lookat), or any 3D node that expects a vec3.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The constant vec3 value with (x, y, z) components. Connect to Bokeh Point, camera nodes, or 3D shape/lighting inputs.

examples: Set (x, y, z) for RGB or position; connect to Output, Mix, Bokeh Point position, or Final Output for a solid color.
Use as light or object position in 3D (e.g. Bokeh Point point input) or as RGB for tinting.
Connect to OKLCH Color Map Bezier or other nodes that take vec3 color or position.

node:constant-vec4.title: Vec4
node:constant-vec4.tagline: Use this to provide constant 4D vectors for RGBA colors, homogeneous coordinates, or 4D data
node:constant-vec4.description: Outputs a constant vec4 value with adjustable x, y, z, and w components. This node is essential for providing fixed 4D values such as RGBA color values, homogeneous coordinates for 3D transformations, or any quadruple of related float values. All four components can be independently adjusted between -1000.0 and 1000.0, with w defaulting to 1.0. Connect to Blend Color or other nodes that accept vec4 for color or homogeneous data.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The constant vec4 value with (x, y, z, w) components. Connect to Blend Color or any vec4 input.

node:oklch-color.title: OKLCH Color
node:oklch-color.tagline: Ideal for creating perceptually uniform colors using the OKLCH color space
node:oklch-color.description: Defines a color value in the OKLCH (OK Lightness, Chroma, Hue) color space, which provides perceptually uniform color representation. This means that equal changes in the L, C, or H values result in visually similar changes in perceived color. The node outputs a vec3 containing (L, C, H) values, where L (lightness) ranges from 0.0 to 1.0, C (chroma/saturation) ranges from 0.0 to 0.4, and H (hue) ranges from 0.0 to 360.0 degrees. This color space is particularly useful for smooth color gradients and color interpolation. Connect to OKLCH Color Map Bezier or OKLCH Color Map Threshold for perceptually smooth gradients from a single value.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: OKLCH color value as (lightness, chroma, hue). Connect to OKLCH Color Map nodes for smooth gradients.

examples: Set L, C, H and connect to OKLCH Color Map Bezier or Output for a base color in a gradient.
Use with Mix or OKLCH Color Map Threshold for perceptually smooth color transitions.
Combine several OKLCH Color nodes in a gradient chain for smooth, uniform steps.
node:oklch-color.advanced: OKLCH is perceptually uniform: equal changes in L, C, or H look equally different to the eye, which makes gradients and interpolation smoother than in RGB.

node:bezier-curve.title: Bezier
node:bezier-curve.tagline: Perfect for defining smooth easing curves for color interpolation and animation timing
node:bezier-curve.description: Defines a cubic Bezier curve for smooth interpolation and easing functions. The node outputs a vec4 containing the two control points (x1, y1, x2, y2) that define the curve's shape. This curve can be used with color mapping nodes to create smooth color transitions, or with animation systems to define easing functions. The curve is visualized in a bezier editor interface where you can drag control points to adjust the curve shape. The curve maps input values (0-1) to output values (0-1) with customizable easing. Connect to OKLCH Color Map Bezier for smooth color ramps, or use with any node that accepts a curve definition.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Bezier curve control points as (x1, y1, x2, y2). Connect to OKLCH Color Map Bezier or other curve-based nodes.

examples: Connect to OKLCH Color Map Bezier as lCurve to shape how a value maps to color (ease-in, ease-out, custom).
Use with Mix for smooth blend curves or with Smoothstep for custom easing.
Drag control points in the editor to design the curve; the output vec4 drives the mapping in color or blend nodes.
node:bezier-curve.advanced: The curve maps 0–1 input to 0–1 output. Use it for easing (slow start/end) or for remapping values before color or blend; linear would be (0,0,1,1).

node:add.title: Add
node:add.tagline: Adds two numbers (or two vec2/vec3/vec4 values). Use it to combine values or add an offset.
node:add.description: Add takes two inputs, a and b, and outputs their sum (a + b). If you leave the second input unconnected, the B parameter is used instead. Handy for shifting a value (e.g. Time + 0.5) or combining two signals.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: A. Connect Time, Float, Sine, or another node’s output.

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: B. If unconnected, the B parameter is used.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The sum a + b. Connect to Multiply, Mix, Translate, or other math/effect nodes.

parameters.name: B
parameters.description: Used for input b when nothing is connected. Set to 0.5 to shift the result by half; use with Time to offset animation phase.

examples: Connect Time to a and a constant to b (or B) to shift animation in time.
Add two Sine waves or two Noise outputs for combined patterns.
Use with Translate: add a vec2 offset to UV for shifting the whole pattern.

node:subtract.title: Subtract
node:subtract.tagline: Ideal for calculating differences and offsets
node:subtract.description: Subtracts the second value from the first. Works with any numeric type through type promotion. Useful for calculating differences, applying negative offsets, and creating distance-based effects. Connect UV to a and a center (e.g. Constant Vec2) to b, then output to Length or Smoothstep for radial falloff; or connect (Noise − 0.5) to Output for centered patterns.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: The value to subtract from. Often UV, Time, or a pattern output.

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: The value to subtract. Often a center point (Constant Vec2), threshold, or offset.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The difference a − b. Connect to Absolute, Length, Output, or Smoothstep for falloffs.

examples: Subtract a center from UV.x or UV.y before Length or Distance for radial effects.
Use (Noise − 0.5) to center a pattern around zero, then feed to Output.
Combine with Absolute to get a distance-like value from a difference.

node:multiply.title: Multiply
node:multiply.tagline: Multiplies two numbers. Use it to scale a value (e.g. slow down or speed up time) or combine signals.
node:multiply.description: Multiply takes two inputs, a and b, and outputs a × b. If b is unconnected, the B parameter is used. Common use: connect Time to a and a small Float to b to slow down an animation, or connect Sine to a and a Float to b to change wave strength.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: A. Often Time, Sine, or a pattern node’s output.

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: B. If unconnected, the B parameter is used.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The product a × b. Connect to Add, Mix, Sine input, or Final Output (floats promote to vec3).

parameters.name: B
parameters.description: Used for input b when nothing is connected. Set to 0.5 to halve the value (e.g. slow down time); set to 2 to double (e.g. brighter or faster).

examples: Connect Time to a and a small constant to b (or B) to slow down animation.
Multiply Sine by a float to change wave strength before Output.
Scale a Noise or Distance value before feeding into Smoothstep or Output.

node:divide.title: Divide
node:divide.tagline: Perfect for normalizing values and calculating ratios
node:divide.description: Divides the first value by the second. Works with any numeric type through type promotion. Useful for normalization, calculating ratios, and inverting scales. Be careful to avoid division by zero. Connect Fragment Coords to a and Resolution to b for aspect-correct normalized coords; or connect Distance to a and a float to b for normalized falloff before Output.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: The dividend (value to be divided). Often Fragment Coords, Distance, or Length.

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: The divisor (value to divide by). Often Resolution or a constant for normalization.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The quotient. Connect to Output, Multiply, or UV-style inputs for normalized values.

node:power.title: Power
node:power.tagline: Ideal for exponential curves and non-linear transformations
node:power.description: Raises the base value to the power of the exponent. Useful for creating exponential curves, gamma corrections, and non-linear transformations. The exponent can be fractional for roots. Connect Smoothstep or Noise to base and a constant to exponent, then to Output for gamma-like falloff; or use exponent 0.5 for square-root style remapping.

inputs.name: base
inputs.description: The base value to raise to a power. Often Smoothstep, Noise, or UV-derived value.

inputs.name: exponent
inputs.description: The exponent to raise the base to. Use a constant (e.g. 2, 0.5) or connect a float.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The result. Connect to Output, Mix, or Clamp for non-linear remapping.

examples: Raise Smoothstep or Noise to an exponent for gamma-like falloff curves.
Use base from UV or pattern and exponent 2 for quadratic falloff.
Power of 0.5 is equivalent to Square Root for non-linear remapping.
node:power.advanced: Fractional exponents give roots (e.g. 0.5 = square root). For e^x use Exponential; for arbitrary base use Power.

node:square-root.title: Square Root
node:square-root.tagline: Use this to calculate square roots and normalize distances
node:square-root.description: Calculates the square root of the input value. Commonly used for distance calculations, normalizing magnitudes, and creating smooth falloff curves. The square root is equivalent to raising a value to the power of 0.5. Connect Length or Distance output to in, then to Smoothstep or Output for soft falloff; or use after Dot Product for magnitude.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The value to calculate the square root of. Often Length, Distance, or Dot Product output.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The square root. Connect to Smoothstep, Mix, or Output for soft falloff.

examples: Feed Length or dot-product result into Square Root for magnitude before Smoothstep.
Use after Distance for softer falloff in radial gradients.
Normalize a squared value (e.g. from Dot Product) back to a length.

node:absolute.title: Absolute
node:absolute.tagline: Perfect for removing negative values and calculating magnitudes
node:absolute.description: Returns the absolute value of the input, removing any negative sign. Useful for distance calculations, ensuring positive values, and working with differences where direction doesn't matter. Connect (UV − 0.5) or (Noise − threshold) to get magnitude for falloff; feed Subtract output here when direction does not matter.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The value. Often Subtract output, Sine, or Noise for magnitude-only use.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The absolute value (always positive or zero). Connect to Multiply, Mix, or Output.

examples: Use on (UV − 0.5) or (Noise − threshold) to get magnitude for falloff.
Feed Subtract output into Absolute when direction does not matter.
Combine with Distance for symmetric masks around a point.

node:floor.title: Floor
node:floor.tagline: Ideal for rounding down to the nearest integer
node:floor.description: Rounds the input value down to the nearest integer. Useful for creating discrete steps, grid-based effects, and quantizing values. Always rounds towards negative infinity. Connect UV × scale or Time to in for discrete cells or frame steps; use with Modulo for repeating tile indices in grids.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The value to round down. Often UV × scale, Time, or Multiply output.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The largest integer less than or equal to the input. Connect to Modulo, Output, or grid nodes.

examples: Floor(UV × scale) or Floor(Time) for discrete cells or frame steps.
Use with Modulo for repeating tile indices in grids.
Quantize a value before Output for stepped gradients.

node:ceil.title: Ceil
node:ceil.tagline: Use this to round up to the nearest integer
node:ceil.description: Rounds the input value up to the nearest integer. Useful for creating discrete steps, grid-based effects, and quantizing values. Always rounds towards positive infinity. Connect Multiply or Divide output to in for ceiling division; use with Modulo or grid nodes for tiling.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The value to round up. Often Multiply, Divide, or UV-derived value.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The smallest integer ≥ input. Connect to Add, Multiply, Modulo, or grid nodes.

node:fract.title: Fract
node:fract.tagline: Perfect for extracting fractional parts and creating repeating patterns
node:fract.description: Returns the fractional part of the input value (the part after the decimal point). Essential for creating repeating patterns, tiling effects, and extracting the decimal portion of values. The result is always between 0.0 and 1.0. Connect UV × scale or Time to in for repeating 0–1 pattern; feed output to Smoothstep, Rings, or Output for tiled effects.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The value. Often UV × scale, Time, or Distance for repeating 0–1.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The fractional part (0–1). Connect to Smoothstep, Rings, Output, or grid nodes.

node:modulo.title: Modulo
node:modulo.tagline: Ideal for creating repeating cycles and wrapping values
node:modulo.description: Calculates the remainder after dividing the first value by the second. Essential for creating repeating patterns, wrapping values within a range, and implementing cyclic behaviors. The result is always between 0.0 and the divisor. Connect Time or UV × scale to a and a constant to b for repeating 0–1; feed output to Sine, Smoothstep, or Rings for cyclic effects.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: The dividend. Often Time, UV, or Multiply for wrapping.

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: The divisor. Usually a constant (e.g. 1 for 0–1 wrap).

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The remainder. Connect to Sine, Smoothstep, Rings, or Output for cycles.

node:min.title: Min
node:min.tagline: Use this to find the smaller of two values
node:min.description: Returns the minimum (smaller) of two input values. Useful for clamping values from above, finding the lower bound, and implementing conditional logic based on value comparisons. Connect a signal (e.g. Noise, distance) to a and an upper limit to b, then output to Output or Mix; often used with Max for soft clamping.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: A — value to compare

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: B — value to compare

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The smaller of the two input values

examples: Min(Noise, 1.0) or Min(distance, radius) to cap from above.
Combine two signals and take the smaller for darken-style blending.
Limit a value before feeding to Mix or Output.

node:max.title: Max
node:max.tagline: Perfect for finding the larger of two values
node:max.description: Returns the maximum (larger) of two input values. Useful for clamping values from below, finding the upper bound, and implementing conditional logic based on value comparisons. Connect a signal to a and a lower limit to b, then to Output or Mix; often used with Min for clamping to a range.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: A — value to compare

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: B — value to compare

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The larger of the two input values

examples: Max(Noise, 0.0) or Max(distance − radius, 0) to clamp from below.
Combine two signals and take the larger for lighten-style blending.
Ensure a value is above a threshold before Smoothstep or Mix.

node:clamp.title: Clamp
node:clamp.tagline: Ideal for constraining values within a specific range
node:clamp.description: Clamps the input value between a minimum and maximum. If the value is below the minimum, it returns the minimum. If above the maximum, it returns the maximum. Otherwise, it returns the original value. Essential for keeping values within valid ranges. Connect Noise, Distance, or Sine to in and set min/max (or connect Float nodes), then to Output or Mix; often used after Smoothstep or Add to keep result in 0–1.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The value to clamp

inputs.name: min
inputs.description: The minimum allowed value

inputs.name: max
inputs.description: The maximum allowed value

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The clamped value (between min and max)

node:mix.title: Mix
node:mix.tagline: Blends two values by a factor: 0 = first, 1 = second. Use for crossfades, masks, and smooth transitions.
node:mix.description: Performs linear interpolation between two values based on a blend factor. When the blend factor is 0.0, it returns the first value. When it's 1.0, it returns the second value. Values in between are linearly interpolated. Connect two values (e.g. two Noise outputs or two colors) and a factor t (e.g. Smoothstep or UV.x); often used when driving Final Output for blended patterns. Works with any numeric type.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: The first value (returned when t = 0.0)

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: The second value (returned when t = 1.0)

inputs.name: t
inputs.description: Interpolation weight (shown as Mix on the node; typically 0.0 to 1.0)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The interpolated value between a and b

examples: Connect two Noise (or Sine) outputs to a and b, and Smoothstep to t for a blended mask.
Crossfade two patterns: one to a, one to b, and a 0–1 factor to t.
Mix two float sources and wire the result to Output (floats promote to vec3).

node:step.title: Step
node:step.tagline: Use this to create hard thresholds and binary decisions
node:step.description: Returns 0.0 if the input value is less than the edge value, otherwise returns 1.0. Creates a hard step function that's useful for creating binary masks, thresholds, and on/off switches based on value comparisons.

inputs.name: edge
inputs.description: The threshold value to compare against

inputs.name: x
inputs.description: The value to compare with the edge

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: 0.0 if x < edge, 1.0 otherwise

examples: Step(threshold, Noise) for a hard cut between two regions.
Create a binary mask from Distance or Length for sharp edges.
Use output as condition for Select or as mask for Mix.
node:step.advanced: Step gives a hard 0/1 boundary; use Smoothstep when you want a smooth transition between two edges.

node:smoothstep.title: Smoothstep
node:smoothstep.tagline: Ideal for creating smooth transitions with ease-in-out curves
node:smoothstep.description: Performs smooth Hermite interpolation between two edge values. Returns 0.0 if the input is below edge0, 1.0 if above edge1, and smoothly interpolates between them. Creates an S-curve that's perfect for smooth transitions, fades, and easing functions.

inputs.name: edge0
inputs.description: The lower edge of the transition (returns 0.0 below this)

inputs.name: edge1
inputs.description: The upper edge of the transition (returns 1.0 above this)

inputs.name: x
inputs.description: The input value to evaluate

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Smoothly interpolated value between 0.0 and 1.0

node:sine.title: Sine
node:sine.tagline: Perfect for creating oscillating waves and periodic animations
node:sine.description: Calculates the sine of the input angle (in radians). Returns values between -1.0 and 1.0, creating a smooth oscillating wave. Essential for creating periodic animations, waves, circular motion, and oscillating effects.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The angle in radians

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The sine value (between -1.0 and 1.0)

node:cosine.title: Cosine
node:cosine.tagline: Use this for oscillating waves with a phase offset
node:cosine.description: Calculates the cosine of the input angle (in radians). Returns values between -1.0 and 1.0, creating a smooth oscillating wave that's phase-shifted by 90 degrees from sine. Useful for circular motion, rotations, and periodic effects.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The angle in radians

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The cosine value (between -1.0 and 1.0)

node:tangent.title: Tangent
node:tangent.tagline: Ideal for calculating slopes and ratios in trigonometry
node:tangent.description: Calculates the tangent of the input angle (in radians). The tangent is the ratio of sine to cosine. Returns values from negative to positive infinity, with discontinuities at odd multiples of π/2. Useful for advanced trigonometric calculations.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The angle in radians

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The tangent value

examples: Tangent(Time) for sharp periodic peaks (clamp input to avoid discontinuities).
Use with Arc Tangent for angle recovery from slope ratios.
Feed normalized angles for repeating saw-like or spike patterns.
node:tangent.advanced: Tangent has discontinuities at ±π/2; clamp input or use modulo to stay within a safe range. Prefer Sine/Cosine for smooth waves.

node:arc-sine.title: Arc Sine
node:arc-sine.tagline: Perfect for converting sine values back to angles
node:arc-sine.description: Calculates the inverse sine (arcsine) of the input value. Returns an angle in radians between -π/2 and π/2. The input must be between -1.0 and 1.0. Useful for converting sine values back to angles and solving trigonometric equations.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The sine value (must be between -1.0 and 1.0)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The angle in radians (between -π/2 and π/2)

node:arc-cosine.title: Arc Cosine
node:arc-cosine.tagline: Use this to convert cosine values back to angles
node:arc-cosine.description: Calculates the inverse cosine (arccosine) of the input value. Returns an angle in radians between 0.0 and π. The input must be between -1.0 and 1.0. Useful for converting cosine values back to angles and solving trigonometric equations.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The cosine value (must be between -1.0 and 1.0)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The angle in radians (between 0.0 and π)

node:arc-tangent.title: Arc Tangent
node:arc-tangent.tagline: Ideal for converting tangent values to angles
node:arc-tangent.description: Calculates the inverse tangent (arctangent) of the input value. Returns an angle in radians between -π/2 and π/2. Useful for converting slopes and ratios back to angles, but limited to two quadrants. For full 360-degree coverage, use Arc Tangent 2.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The tangent value (ratio)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The angle in radians (between -π/2 and π/2)

node:arc-tangent-2.title: Arc Tangent 2
node:arc-tangent-2.tagline: Perfect for converting coordinates to angles with full quadrant support
node:arc-tangent-2.description: Calculates the angle from the x-axis to a point given by (x, y) coordinates. Returns an angle in radians between -π and π, covering all four quadrants. Essential for converting 2D coordinates to angles, calculating directions, and working with polar coordinates.

inputs.name: y
inputs.description: The y-coordinate of the point

inputs.name: x
inputs.description: The x-coordinate of the point

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The angle in radians (between -π and π)

node:exponential.title: Exponential
node:exponential.tagline: Use this for exponential growth and decay curves
node:exponential.description: Calculates e (Euler's number, approximately 2.718) raised to the power of the input. Creates exponential growth curves when the input is positive and exponential decay when negative. Useful for natural growth/decay effects, logarithmic scales, and advanced mathematical operations.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The exponent to raise e to

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: e raised to the power of the input

node:natural-logarithm.title: Natural Logarithm
node:natural-logarithm.tagline: Ideal for inverting exponential functions and logarithmic scales
node:natural-logarithm.description: Calculates the natural logarithm (base e) of the input value. The input must be greater than zero. This is the inverse of the exponential function. Useful for compressing large value ranges, inverting exponential curves, and working with logarithmic scales.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The value to calculate the logarithm of (must be > 0)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The natural logarithm of the input

node:length.title: Length
node:length.tagline: Perfect for calculating vector magnitudes and distances from origin
node:length.description: Calculates the length (magnitude) of a vector. For a 2D vector, this is the distance from the origin (0,0) to the point. Essential for distance calculations, normalizing vectors, and working with vector magnitudes. Connect UV or Translate output to in, then to Smoothstep or Output for radial falloff from center; often used with Distance and Square Root for magnitude-based effects.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The vector. Often UV, Translate output, or position for radial falloff.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Magnitude (length). Connect to Smoothstep, Output, or Square Root for falloff.

node:distance.title: Distance
node:distance.tagline: Use this to calculate the distance between two points
node:distance.description: Calculates the Euclidean distance between two points. Returns the length of the vector from point a to point b. Essential for proximity-based effects, radial gradients, and spatial calculations. Connect UV to a and Constant Vec2 (e.g. 0.5, 0.5) to b, then output to Smoothstep or Output for radial falloff; often used with Length and Normalize for direction-based effects.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: The first point. Often UV, Translate output, or center.

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: The second point. Often Constant Vec2 (center) or another UV/position.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Distance between a and b. Connect to Smoothstep, Output, or Rings for radial effects.

node:dot-product.title: Dot Product
node:dot-product.tagline: Ideal for calculating angles, projections, and lighting
node:dot-product.description: Calculates the dot product of two vectors. Returns a scalar value that represents the cosine of the angle between vectors (when normalized) multiplied by their lengths. Essential for lighting calculations, angle measurements, and vector projections. Connect Normalize(UV) to a and a direction (e.g. Constant Vec2) to b, then to Output or Lighting Shading; often used with Normalize for lighting and reflections.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: The first vector. Often Normalize(UV) or a direction from a node.

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: The second vector. Often Normalize output, Normal Mapping, or Constant Vec2.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Dot product (scalar). Connect to Lighting Shading, Mix, Smoothstep, or Output.

node:cross-product.title: Cross Product
node:cross-product.tagline: Perfect for calculating perpendicular vectors and surface normals
node:cross-product.description: Calculates the cross product of two 3D vectors. Returns a vector perpendicular to both input vectors, with magnitude equal to the area of the parallelogram they form. Essential for calculating surface normals, determining orientation, and 3D vector mathematics. Connect two tangent or edge vectors to a and b, then output to Normalize for a surface normal; often used with Normal Mapping and Lighting Shading in 3D.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: The first 3D vector. Often Normalize or Normal Mapping output.

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: The second 3D vector. Often Normalize or Normal Mapping output.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Cross product (perpendicular vector). Connect to Normalize, Normal Mapping, or Lighting Shading.

node:normalize.title: Normalize
node:normalize.tagline: Use this to create unit vectors with length 1.0
node:normalize.description: Normalizes a vector to unit length (magnitude of 1.0) while preserving its direction. The result points in the same direction as the input but has a length of exactly 1.0. Essential for direction vectors, normals, and any calculations requiring unit vectors. Connect UV or a direction to in, then output to Dot Product or Reflect for lighting/reflections; often used with Length and Distance for direction-only math.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The vector to normalize. Often UV, Translate output, or polar/position vector.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Unit vector (length 1). Connect to Dot Product, Reflect, Refract, or Lighting Shading.

node:reflect.title: Reflect
node:reflect.tagline: Ideal for calculating reflection vectors for lighting and mirrors
node:reflect.description: Calculates the reflection of an incident vector off a surface with a given normal. The result is the direction the vector would bounce after hitting the surface. Essential for specular reflections, mirror effects, and physically-based lighting calculations. Connect view or light direction to I and surface normal to N, then output to Dot Product or Lighting Shading for specular; often used with Normalize.

inputs.name: I
inputs.description: Incident direction. Often Normalize(UV) or view/light direction.

inputs.name: N
inputs.description: Surface normal (normalized). Often from Normal Mapping or Constant Vec2.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Reflected direction. Connect to Dot Product or Lighting Shading for specular.

node:refract.title: Refract
node:refract.tagline: Perfect for calculating refraction through transparent materials
node:refract.description: Calculates the refracted vector when light passes through a surface with different indices of refraction. The eta parameter is the ratio of the first medium's index to the second medium's index. Essential for realistic glass, water, and transparent material effects. Connect view direction to I, surface normal to N, and ratio (e.g. 0.75 for air/glass) to eta; output to Dot Product or Lighting Shading for refraction. Often used with Normalize.

inputs.name: I
inputs.description: Incident direction (entering surface). Often Normalize(UV) or view direction.

inputs.name: N
inputs.description: Surface normal (normalized). Often from Normal Mapping or Constant Vec2.

inputs.name: eta
inputs.description: Ratio n1/n2 (e.g. 0.75 for air→glass). Connect Constant Float or Divide.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Refracted direction. Connect to Normalize, Dot Product, or Lighting Shading.

node:blend-mode.title: Blend Channel
node:blend-mode.tagline: Ideal for combining two float values with multiply, screen, overlay, and other blend modes
node:blend-mode.description: Applies Photoshop-style blending to two scalar (float) signals—for example luminance, masks, or values you later feed through Output or OKLCH maps. Twelve modes are available: Normal (the Blend input as the blend side, then Opacity mixes toward Background), Multiply, Screen, Overlay, Soft Light, Hard Light, Color Dodge, Color Burn, Linear Dodge (Add), Linear Burn, Difference, and Exclusion. Opacity mixes the blended result back toward the base. If either input is unwired, it uses the default typed-zero value.

inputs.name: base
inputs.description: Background value to blend onto. When unwired, defaults to 0 (often reads as black in a float-only chain to Output).

inputs.name: blend
inputs.description: Foreground value blended over the base using the selected mode. When unwired, it defaults to 0.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Blended float result, mixed toward the base by Opacity. Often chain to Final Output (floats promote to vec3), or to Mix/color grading.

examples: Connect two patterns or masks to Background and Blend, pick a mode, then lower Opacity for a subtle mix.
Chain UV → Noise → Blend (foreground) with a second source on Background; wire Blend out to Final Output for color.
If Background is unwired, the blend runs against 0—expect a dark bias unless you drive Background.
node:blend-mode.advanced: For two full RGBA layers you wire yourself, use Blend Color (same mode list, per-channel). Use Blend Channel when both operands are floats (e.g. before Final Output).

node:blend-color.title: Blend Color
node:blend-color.tagline: Blend two RGBA colors with Photoshop-style modes, per channel—same semantics as Blend Channel but on vec4
node:blend-color.description: Takes Background and Blend as vec4 RGBA. Each RGB channel uses the selected blend mode with the same order as Blend Channel (Normal uses each channel of the Blend input as the blend-side value; Multiply through Exclusion follow Photoshop-style per-channel math). Opacity interpolates blended RGB toward the Background RGB (not Porter–Duff composite). Alpha is interpolated between Background.a and Blend.a by the same Opacity (straight alpha semantics; not multiplied into RGB). When a port is unwired, it defaults to vec4(0.0)—same typed-zero convention as other vec4 inputs.

inputs.name: base
inputs.description: Background color

inputs.name: blend
inputs.description: Blend-layer color blended per channel onto Background

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Blended RGBA

examples: Chain two color pipelines into Background and Blend, pick Overlay or Soft Light, then reduce Opacity for a tinted photo-style mix.
Use Normal with Opacity below 1 for a simpler linear RGB mix without per-channel Photoshop math.
node:blend-color.advanced: Mode indices match Blend Channel (scalar node): 0=Normal/pass channel, …, 11=Exclusion. This is linear channel math in the shader’s working space, not automated sRGB decode/encode.

node:oklch-color-map-bezier.title: Color Map Smooth
node:oklch-color-map-bezier.tagline: Map a 0–1 value to RGB through OKLCH with smooth easing on L, chroma (C), and hue (H) via independent cubic Bézier curves
node:oklch-color-map-bezier.description: Converts a float value (0–1) to an RGB color using the OKLCH color space. Start and end colors are taken from the OKLCH Color (or compatible vec3) ports when wired; otherwise the panel lightness, chroma, and hue fields are used as L, C, and H endpoints. Separate cubic Bézier curves control easing for each dimension before conversion to RGB. Use Reverse hue to pick the complementary wrap direction across the hue ring when interpolating.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input value (0–1) to map to a color

inputs.name: startColor
inputs.description: Starting color as OKLCH in vec3.xyz = (Lightness 0–1, Chroma, Hue degrees)—for example OKLCH Color out; used when wired; sliders apply when unwired.

inputs.name: endColor
inputs.description: Ending color as OKLCH in vec3.xyz = (Lightness 0–1, Chroma, Hue degrees)—for example OKLCH Color out; used when wired; sliders apply when unwired.

inputs.name: lCurve
inputs.description: Cubic bezier control points for Lightness interpolation (x1, y1, x2, y2)

inputs.name: cCurve
inputs.description: Cubic bezier control points for Chroma interpolation (x1, y1, x2, y2)

inputs.name: hCurve
inputs.description: Cubic bezier control points for Hue interpolation (x1, y1, x2, y2)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The resulting RGB color converted from OKLCH

node:oklch-color-map-threshold.title: Color Map Stepped
node:oklch-color-map-threshold.tagline: Ideal for creating color gradients with discrete color stops and optional dithering using OKLCH color space
node:oklch-color-map-threshold.description: Converts a float value (0-1) to an RGB color using the OKLCH color space with threshold-based color stops. This mode creates discrete color bands with configurable transition widths between stops, allowing for stepped gradients or posterized color effects. Optional Bayer dithering can be applied to reduce banding artifacts. The node generates color stops on-the-fly based on the stops parameter, interpolating between start and end colors using bezier curves for L, C, and H components. The result is converted from OKLCH to RGB for display.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input value (0-1) to map to a color

inputs.name: startColor
inputs.description: The starting OKLCH color (L, C, H components)

inputs.name: endColor
inputs.description: The ending OKLCH color (L, C, H components)

inputs.name: lCurve
inputs.description: Cubic bezier control points for Lightness interpolation (x1, y1, x2, y2)

inputs.name: cCurve
inputs.description: Cubic bezier control points for Chroma interpolation (x1, y1, x2, y2)

inputs.name: hCurve
inputs.description: Cubic bezier control points for Hue interpolation (x1, y1, x2, y2)

inputs.name: fragCoord
inputs.description: Fragment coordinates for dithering calculations

inputs.name: resolution
inputs.description: Screen resolution for dithering calculations

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The resulting RGB color converted from OKLCH with optional dithering applied

examples: Feed Noise or Smoothstep into Color Map Stepped for discrete color bands or posterized gradients.
Enable dithering and connect Frag Coord and Resolution to reduce banding in gradients.
Chain: pattern → Color Map Stepped → Glow or Color Grading for stylized color looks.
node:oklch-color-map-threshold.advanced: Threshold mode creates discrete stops; use the stops parameter to control how many bands. Connect Bayer Dither upstream or use the node's built-in dither option to reduce banding on limited color depth.

node:glow-bloom.title: Glow
node:glow-bloom.tagline: Bloom from bright passes: halo around highlights (full blur on WebGPU; fast preview elsewhere)
node:glow-bloom.description: **WebGPU** path: threshold masked bright pixels, blur them wide, then add back—a real-ish bloom spill. **WebGL preview**: bright-pass on **Rec.709 luminance** preserves hue, then Strength × a Radius-aware spread boost approximates softer bloom until multipass kicks in. Connect vec4 chains into **Color** toward **Final Output** or downstream grade.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input color to glow. Connect vec4 from Mix, Blend, OKLCH color-map nodes, or other color chains here.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The color with glow and bloom effects applied

examples: Chain: UV → Noise → Output → Glow → Output for a glowing grayscale pattern.
Add glow after Mix or Blend to make bright blended areas bloom.
Feed Glow output into Edge Detection or Color Grading for stylized looks.
node:glow-bloom.advanced: WebGPU consumes Radius in the Gaussian separable blur pass; preview paths lean on Threshold/Intensity/Strength with Radius as spread hint.

node:blur.title: Blur
node:blur.tagline: Gaussian / directional / radial separable blur (WebGPU)—WebGL previews with weighted luminance soften
node:blur.description: **WebGPU** treats this node like **Bokeh** and **Glow**: it uses a **real separable Gaussian** pass plan when your chain is … → **Blur** → **Final Output**. Wire **Blur into another node** (e.g. **Blend**, **Mix**, **Glow**) and WebGPU uses the same **single-pass approximation** as the WebGL preview so previews stay responsive. **WebGL** cannot sample neighborhoods: Amount turns the effect **on/off** (gates how much softened luminance mixes in), Radius scales blur strength once Amount is raised, Mode picks **Gaussian**, **Directional**, or **Radial**, and Direction / radial center tweak the approximation so it tracks the eventual WebGPU blur.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Scene or graded color you want softened. Typical: … → Output → Blur → Final Output.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Blurred RGB (Gaussian on WebGPU when directly before Final Output). Alpha preserved where supported.

parameters.name: Amount
parameters.description: Blur mix: raise this to reveal blur. Matches the WebGPU pass blend toward blurred output—when Amount is 0 you see no blur even if Radius is high.

parameters.name: Radius
parameters.description: How strong the blur is once Amount > 0. On WebGPU the kernel spread grows with Radius (pixels) times Amount.

parameters.name: Mode
parameters.description: Gaussian blur in all directions, Directional streak along Angle, or Radial blur outward from radial center.

parameters.name: Direction
parameters.description: Angle in degrees for Directional mode: controls streak direction relative to screen space.

parameters.name: Center X
parameters.description: Horizontal offset from canvas center along UV (paired with Center Y): WebGPU radial mode samples toward point **0.5 + 0.5 × this** so (0,0) keeps the bloom center mid-frame.

parameters.name: Center Y
parameters.description: Vertical offset like Center X—slide toward ±2 along each axis to move the radial focal point off-center.

examples: Soften procedural noise before output: Noise → Output → Blur (Gaussian) → Final Output on WebGPU for true neighbor blur.
Directional mode + Direction for horizontal film streak or motion-blur vibes; dial Amount then Radius.
Radial mode: set Center slightly off-axis for asymmetric smear toward an edge focal point.
Chain Blur into Mix or Glow when you want a lighter inline blur—expect a softer preview match until you reconnect before Final Output.
node:blur.advanced: **Coordinate note (radial):** Center axes use the WGSL radial mapping **UV focal = (0.5, 0.5) + 0.5 × (Center X, Center Y)** inside 0–1 UV—roughly ±1 spans the plane from center toward edges; widen the Range if you deliberately overshoot.

node:edge-detection.title: Edge Detection
node:edge-detection.tagline: Threshold ridge on luminance—not full Sobel / neighbor convolution
node:edge-detection.description: Builds an **edge mask** from distance of **Rec.709 luminance** to **Threshold**, smoothed by **Width**, then mixes toward **Intensity** with **Strength**. Output **preserves saturation** by scaling RGB with the ridge factor. Useful for illustrative outlines where you want perceptual breaks, not convolution-accurate image edges.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input color to detect edges in

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The color with edge detection effects applied

node:chromatic-aberration.title: Chromatic Aberration
node:chromatic-aberration.tagline: Radial lens-style RGB tilt from center—Direction rotates the fringe axis
node:chromatic-aberration.description: **Screenspace modulation** (no per-channel UV resampling): from each pixel, boosts/cuts R/B along a radial vector projected onto **Direction** (degrees). **Strength** scales separation, **Falloff** damps farther from center, center offsets sit in normalized screen space—good for prismy edges or subtle lens quirks. Differs from **RGB Separation**, which biases channels with planar dot offsets.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input color to apply chromatic aberration to

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The color with RGB channel separation applied

node:color-grading.title: Color Grading
node:color-grading.tagline: Lift-style RGB vs luminance rails + Photoshop-like levels lift/gamma gains
node:color-grading.description: Shadows/Midtones/Highlights are **per-channel multiply masks** keyed off smooth luminance bands (not Bézier spline UI)—think lift/gamma without curve editors. **Levels** remap each channel independently (black/white in & out plus gamma exponent). Clamp-friendly for 0–1 chains; upstream HDR-ish values should be toned down first.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input color to apply color grading to

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The color-graded output with adjusted curves and levels

examples: Chain Glow → Color Grading → Tone Mapping → Output for full control over shadows, midtones, and highlights.
Use after post-process (Blur, Edge Detection) to warm or cool the final image or adjust contrast curves.
Combine with OKLCH Color Map nodes: grade the result for film-like or stylized color before Output.

node:rgb-separation.title: RGB Separation
node:rgb-separation.tagline: Glitch tint: planar dot-offset per RGB channel against aspect-correct screen coords
node:rgb-separation.description: Each channel scales by `1 + k * dot(p, channelOffset)` on **aspect-corrected** fragment space—**not** dragging UV lookups apart. Labels **Rx/Ry**, **Gx/Gy**, **Bx/By** are the per-axis weights; **Strength** scales **k**. Pairs nicely with CRT scanlines because it exaggerates fringe without resampling upstream textures differently per channel.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input color to separate RGB channels from

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The color with RGB channel separation applied

examples: Connect Glow → RGB Separation → Output for per-channel offset glitch or VHS-style artifacts.
Set different Rx/Ry/Gx/Gy/Bx/By biases per channel; chain before or after Chromatic Aberration for layered color effects.
Use strength to control intensity; combine with Scanlines for full retro display look.

node:scanlines.title: Scanlines
node:scanlines.tagline: CRT-style horizontal matte bars driven by sine wave density + optional scroll
node:scanlines.description: **Frequency** scales the sine phase along **aspect-correct Y** — higher reads as tighter stripes. Bars are hardened with **Thickness** then applied on **Rec.709 luminance** while **preserving saturation** via per-channel ratios. **Time speed** × (**time + Time offset**) scrolls stripes; intensity still keys off luminance unlike per-RGB CRT phosphor sims.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input color to apply scanlines to

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The color with scanline overlay applied

examples: Connect Chromatic Aberration or graded color → Scanlines → Output for CRT or retro display style.
Drive time into the node (or use time speed parameter) to animate scrolling scanlines.
Chain with RGB Separation or Glow for layered vintage or glitch aesthetics.

node:block-edge-brightness.title: Edge Brightness
node:block-edge-brightness.tagline: Ideal for adding brightness variation at block edges, typically used with block-displacement for glitch effects
node:block-edge-brightness.description: Adds brightness variation at block edges, creating visual emphasis on block boundaries. This effect is designed to work in conjunction with block-displacement nodes, matching their block structure parameters (count, direction, spacing chaos) to create cohesive glitch effects. The node detects block boundaries and applies configurable brightness adjustments at the edges, with smooth transitions controlled by the edge width parameter. This creates a distinctive glitch aesthetic where block boundaries are highlighted, enhancing the visual impact of block-based distortions. The effect supports both horizontal and vertical block orientations.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input color to apply block edge brightness to

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The color with brightness variation at block edges

node:block-color-glitch.title: Block Color Glitch
node:block-color-glitch.tagline: Perfect for applying color-based visual glitches using block structure for digital artifact effects
node:block-color-glitch.description: Applies color-based visual glitches using a block structure system. The node can operate in two modes: pre-color-mapping (modifies the result value before color mapping) or post-color-mapping (modifies RGB colors after color mapping). It offers multiple glitch effects including invert, brightness shift, threshold offset, RGB separation, hue rotation, saturation shift, color tint, and noise injection. The block parameters are automatically synced from block-displacement nodes when present. You can select which blocks are affected (all, small only, or large only) and control the intensity and amount of each effect. This creates sophisticated digital glitch aesthetics with precise control over the visual artifacts.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input color to apply block glitch effects to

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The color with block-based glitch effects applied

node:normal-mapping.title: Normal Mapping
node:normal-mapping.tagline: Screenspace bumps from luminance + simple light (approximate embossed look)
node:normal-mapping.description: No height texture taps: derives a dome-like tangent from aspect-correct **XY** multiplied by **Scale**, with slope amplitude tied to Rec.709 **luminance** from the RGBA input. Applies a half-Lambert style term from **Lighting** knobs, mixes by **Strength**, then scales RGB preserving hue/saturation ratios. Useful for embossed highlights on fullscreen passes—not PBR tangent-space normals.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: RGBA source; shading keys off luminance encoded in RGB

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The color with normal mapping and lighting applied

node:lighting-shading.title: Lighting
node:lighting-shading.tagline: Screenspace Lambert-ish tint on HDR-ish luminance—not a physically lit mesh
node:lighting-shading.description: **Directional** derives a positional fake-normal (same scaffolding as Normal Mapping-lite) then Lambert against your light vector. **Point** mode returns inverse-square attenuation-only (no cosine term). Shading multiplies preserved RGB by **ambient + attenuation**, then scales by tinted **RGB** sliders. Interpret as stylized modulation on fullscreen color, not a complete PBR shading model.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: RGBA color stream; computations use luminance-derived carriers

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The color with lighting and shading applied

examples: Connect Normal Mapping (or a lit color chain) → Lighting → Output to add directional or point light and depth.
Chain: pattern → Output → Lighting; set light type (directional or point) and direction/position for 3D feel.
Use with Normal Mapping: feed lit result back into Normal Mapping or Mix for layered shading.

node:rotate.title: Rotate
node:rotate.tagline: Ideal for rotating coordinates around a center point by a specified angle
node:rotate.description: Rotates 2D coordinates around a specified center point by a given angle in radians. Uses standard rotation matrix calculations to transform the input coordinates. The rotation is counter-clockwise for positive angles. You can control both the rotation angle and the pivot point (center) of rotation.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input 2D coordinates to rotate

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The rotated coordinates around the specified center point

examples: Feed UV into Rotate then Noise and Output for a rotated pattern; drive angle with Time for spinning.
Use with Polar Coords and Kaleidoscope for radial symmetry, or with Displace to place the center.
Connect Sine(Time) to a center or angle for animated rotation.
node:rotate.advanced: Angle is in radians; use Sine/Cosine of Time for smooth rotation. Rotate before pattern nodes so the whole pattern turns around the center.

node:scale.title: Scale
node:scale.tagline: Makes a pattern bigger or smaller by scaling the coordinates. Scale X and Scale Y control size; Center X/Y set the point everything scales around.
node:scale.description: Scale takes a vec2 (e.g. from UV Coords) and multiplies the distance from a center point. Scale X and Scale Y above 1 make the pattern repeat more (smaller tiles); below 1 make it stretch (fewer, bigger repeats). Center X and Center Y set the point around which scaling happens (0.5, 0.5 is the middle of the screen).

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: 2D coordinates to scale. Usually UV Coords or Translate/Rotate output.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Scaled coordinates. Connect to Noise, Translate, or other vec2 inputs.

parameters.name: Scale X
parameters.description: How much to stretch coordinates horizontally. Higher = smaller repeating pattern (more tiles); lower = stretched, fewer repeats.

parameters.name: Scale Y
parameters.description: How much to stretch coordinates vertically. Higher = more vertical repeats; lower = stretched vertically.

parameters.name: Center X
parameters.description: Horizontal position of the center point that scaling is relative to. 0.5 = middle of screen; change to zoom from a different point.

parameters.name: Center Y
parameters.description: Vertical position of the center point that scaling is relative to. 0.5 = middle of screen; change to zoom from a different point.

node:polar-coordinates.title: Polar Coords
node:polar-coordinates.tagline: Perfect for converting Cartesian coordinates to polar coordinates, enabling radial and rotational effects
node:polar-coordinates.description: Converts 2D Cartesian coordinates to polar coordinates (angle and radius), applies scaling and rotation transformations, then converts back to Cartesian. This enables radial effects, circular patterns, and rotational distortions. You can control the center point, angular scale, radial scale, and rotation. The conversion allows for effects that are naturally expressed in polar space, such as radial gradients, circular patterns, and rotational symmetries.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input 2D Cartesian coordinates to convert

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The transformed coordinates after polar conversion, scaling, and rotation

examples: Connect UV → Polar Coords → Rings (or Length → Output) for radial patterns and circular gradients.
Use before Kaleidoscope or Twist for rotational symmetry; adjust center and scales to match your layout.
Feed into Arc Tangent 2 and Length for angle/radius; combine with Noise for polar-distorted noise.
node:polar-coordinates.advanced: Converts (x,y) to angle and radius, scales and rotates, then back to Cartesian. Use for any effect that is easier in polar space (rings, spirals, radial blur).

node:kaleidoscope.title: Kaleidoscope
node:kaleidoscope.tagline: Symmetric mirrors around a center; optional smooth edge softens folds
node:kaleidoscope.description: Creates kaleidoscope effects by dividing the coordinate space into segments and folding each segment for radial symmetry. At Smooth Edge 0 the fold is a hard mirror (classic kaleidoscope); raise Smooth Edge to blend across segment midlines for softer symmetry like the old Kaleidoscope Smooth node. Control segment count (2–32), center, rotation, and smooth edge width.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input 2D coordinates to apply kaleidoscope transformation to

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The mirrored coordinates creating symmetric kaleidoscope patterns

parameters.name: Smooth Edge
