parameters.description: 0 = sharp mirror fold between segments. Larger values blend the reflection across the fold line for softer symmetry.

examples: Feed UV or Polar Coords into Kaleidoscope, then Noise or Length → Output for symmetric patterns.
Increase segments for more mirrors (e.g. 6 for hex symmetry); rotate for animated kaleidoscope.
Combine with Rings or Radial Rays for mandala-style effects.
node:kaleidoscope.advanced: Segments divide the circle; each segment is folded. Smooth Edge uses smoothstep around the mid-segment angle; use 0 for identical behavior to the legacy hard-fold kaleidoscope.

node:turbulence.title: Turbulence
node:turbulence.tagline: Perfect for applying multi-stage coordinate warping to create complex, organic distortions
node:turbulence.description: Applies multi-stage domain warping using iterative noise-based distortions. The node performs multiple iterations of noise sampling at different scales, accumulating distortions to create complex, organic-looking warping effects. Each iteration uses a different scale (powers of 2), creating fractal-like distortion patterns. The effect can be animated over time and controlled through scale, strength, number of iterations, and time-based parameters. This creates fluid, turbulent distortions similar to water, smoke, or heat haze effects.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input 2D coordinates to apply turbulence distortion to

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The warped coordinates after multi-stage turbulence distortion

examples: Connect UV → Turbulence → Noise or Length → Output for organic, fluid-like distortion.
Drive time speed with Time for animated turbulence; increase iterations for more complexity.
Feed Turbulence output into Displace or another pattern for layered warping.
node:turbulence.advanced: Multi-octave domain warping; each iteration samples noise at different scales. More iterations add detail but cost; use for heat haze, water, or abstract flow. Time Speed spans a wide range: low values give slow, subtle drift; higher values restore intense, grungy motion.

node:vector-field.title: Vector Field
node:vector-field.tagline: Ideal for creating warping effects using a vector field that distorts UV coordinates
node:vector-field.description: Applies vector field-based distortion to coordinates, creating complex warping effects. The node generates a vector field using trigonometric functions with configurable frequencies, then uses this field to offset coordinates. The vector field includes harmonic layers for complexity, radial strength for distance-based effects, and can be animated over time. The distortion is calculated using dot products and cross products (2D equivalent) to create directional warping. This creates flowing, dynamic distortions that can simulate fluid motion, magnetic fields, or abstract warping effects.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input 2D coordinates to apply vector field distortion to

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The distorted coordinates after vector field transformation

examples: Connect UV → Vector Field → Noise or Length → Output for flowing, directional warping.
Animate with Time for moving flow; tune frequency and strength for subtle or strong distortion.
Chain with Turbulence for complex organic motion.
node:vector-field.advanced: Vector field offsets coordinates using trig-based directions. Use Radial Strength for distance-based effect; combine with Turbulence for layered distortion.

node:radial-uv-warp.title: Radial Warp
node:radial-uv-warp.tagline: Bulge/pinch, fisheye (lens), or spherize (hemisphere)—one node, pick a Mode
node:radial-uv-warp.description: Shared center (X/Y) plus a Mode selects the warp: **Bulge / pinch** (localized radial warp with radius and falloff), **Fisheye** (global lens barrel/pincushion with aspect correction), **Spherize** (rectangle-to-hemisphere for ball/planet-style mapping). Older graphs that used separate Bulge/Pinch, Fisheye, or Spherize nodes are not migrated—add this node instead.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input coordinates

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Warped coordinates

node:ripple.title: Ripple
node:ripple.tagline: Sine-wave displacement; concentric or directional for water/glass effects
node:ripple.description: Displaces coordinates with sine waves. Concentric mode creates circular ripples from a center; Directional mode creates a grid of waves. Frequency, amplitude, phase and optional time speed control the effect.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input coordinates

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Rippled coordinates

node:mirror-flip.title: Flip
node:mirror-flip.description: Flips input coordinates around an optional center. Enable Flip X and/or Flip Y for mirroring. Useful for symmetry and avoiding duplicate artwork.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input coordinates

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Flipped coordinates

examples: Flip X and/or Y for mirror symmetry; connect UV → Flip → pattern for reflected layout.
Use with Kaleidoscope or tiling patterns to avoid drawing both sides of a symmetric design.
Set center for flip-around-point instead of screen edges.
node:mirror-flip.advanced: Flips coordinates; no scaling. Use before pattern nodes so the pattern itself is mirrored. Combine with Scale for flip then zoom.

node:displace.title: Displace
node:displace.tagline: Pan or warp UVs: vector offset from parameters or directional scalar smear
node:displace.description: Mode Vector (0): adds vec2(Offset X, Offset Y) × Scale to the UV input (same idea as the old Translate node). Mode Directional (1): moves along Angle by Amount × Scale (same as the old Directional Displace). For animated or procedural offsets, build them upstream of the UV input (e.g. Combine Vector, noise) rather than a separate offset port.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input coordinates (e.g. UV).

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Displaced coordinates

parameters.name: Mode
parameters.description: Vector: in + vec2(Offset X, Offset Y) × Scale. Directional: in + (cos Angle, sin Angle) × Amount × Scale.

parameters.name: Scale
parameters.description: Multiplies the displacement in both modes.

parameters.name: Offset X / Y
parameters.description: Vector mode only: fixed pan / warp offset before Scale.

parameters.name: Angle
parameters.description: Direction of displacement in Directional mode (radians).

parameters.name: Amount
parameters.description: Directional mode only: scalar distance before Scale.

examples: Vector mode: UV → Vortex → Displace; tune Offset X/Y (and Scale) for pan and extra warp after the vortex.
Vector mode: UV → Displace → Noise; drag Offset X/Y to pan (old Translate workflow).
Vector mode: UV → Combine Vector (or noise) → Displace UV input to add procedural offset in the coordinate chain.
Directional mode: set Mode to Directional, set Angle and Amount (and Scale) for a directional smear.
node:displace.advanced: Vector mode formula: out = in + vec2(Offset X, Offset Y) * Scale. Directional: out = in + dir(angle) * (Amount * Scale). Legacy graphs load via migration from translate / directional-displace; wires to removed offset/amount ports are dropped on load.

node:vortex.title: Vortex
node:vortex.tagline: Spiral UV warp: rotate around a center; radial pull adds inward suction
node:vortex.description: Pulls and rotates coordinates around a center. Strength, radius, and falloff shape the effect; Time Speed spins the pattern. Radial pull is 0–1: at 0, UV rotates without shrinking toward the center (classic twist); at 1 (default), coordinates spiral inward for drain/portal looks.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input coordinates

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Vortex-distorted coordinates

examples: Connect UV or Polar Coords → Vortex → Noise or Length → Output for drain or portal spin.
Animate time speed for rotating vortex; tune radius and falloff to limit the spiral region.
Set radial pull to 0 for rotation-only warp; combine with Ripple or chain two Vortex nodes for layered spirals.
node:vortex.advanced: Radial pull scales the inward spiral squeeze; at 0 the warp is angular only. Strength and radius set how tight the swirl is.

node:quad-warp.title: Quad Warp
node:quad-warp.tagline: Map unit square to a quadrilateral for perspective/screen effects
node:quad-warp.description: Maps input (u,v) from the unit square to a quadrilateral defined by four corners (BL, BR, TL, TR). Use for perspective distortion, screens, and poster effects.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input coordinates (e.g. 0–1)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Warped coordinates

examples: Connect UV → Quad Warp → Noise or Length → Output for perspective or screen distortion.
Set the four corners (BL, BR, TL, TR) to shape the quad; use for fake 3D planes or posters.
Animate corners with Time or Sine for warping screens or flags.
node:quad-warp.advanced: Bilinear map from unit square to quadrilateral. Use for perspective correction, TV screens, or any four-corner warp.

node:brick-tiling.title: Tiling
node:brick-tiling.tagline: Tile UVs for seamless repeats (optional brick stagger)
node:brick-tiling.description: Tiles 2D coordinates so downstream patterns repeat seamlessly. Scale X/Y control the tile count; Offset X/Y shift the tile phase. For brick/staggered tiling, turn up Brick Amount and set Brick Offset X (try ~0.5 for classic brick).

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input coordinates

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Tiled coordinates (0–1 per tile)

parameters.name: Scale X / Y
parameters.description: How many tiles you get across X and Y.

parameters.name: Offset X / Y
parameters.description: Shifts the tiling phase (still wraps per tile).

parameters.name: Brick Amount
parameters.description: 0 = plain tiling. 1 = full brick stagger.

parameters.name: Brick Offset X
parameters.description: Horizontal offset applied to every other row when Brick Amount > 0. Try ~0.5 for classic brick.

node:infinite-zoom.title: Infinite Zoom
node:infinite-zoom.tagline: Folded UV tunnel zoom — ping-pong loop or snap ramp then reset
node:infinite-zoom.description: Uses scene time with Cycle length (seconds). Motion chooses Ping-pong loop (smooth zoom in then out each cycle), Snap zoom in (ramp from neutral to max zoom then jump back), or Snap zoom out (start zoomed in, ramp out, then jump back). Zoom step is a gentle per-layer multiplier (narrow range). Depth scales how far into the fold each cycle reaches. UVs are scaled, folded with fract into a repeating cell, then the center is reapplied. Legacy graphs with Scale / Time Speed migrate automatically.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input UV

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Zoomed/wrapped coordinates

node:noise.title: Noise
node:noise.tagline: Creates a random-looking pattern from coordinates. Feed it UV (or translated/scaled UV) and get a float you can turn into color or use for effects.
node:noise.description: Noise takes a vec2 (usually from UV Coords) and outputs a float that looks random but is smooth and repeatable. You can choose the style (Mode), change how big or detailed the pattern is (Scale, Octaves), and animate it with Time. Connect the output to Output to see it as grayscale (floats promote to vec3), or use it to drive other effects.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Position to sample noise at. Connect UV Coords, or Translate/Scale for shifted or zoomed noise.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Noise value (about 0–1). Connect to Output to see it, or to Mix/Smoothstep for effects.

parameters.name: Mode
parameters.description: Noise style: Simplex 2D (smooth 2D), Simplex 3D (smoother, can animate with time), or Value fBm (more jagged, terrain-like). Simplex 2D/3D look smoother; Value fBm looks more rough and layered.

parameters.name: Scale
parameters.description: How big the noise features are. Higher = larger blobs and smoother variation; lower = finer grain.

parameters.name: Octaves
parameters.description: How many layers of detail are combined. More octaves = finer, more detailed pattern; fewer = simpler, softer look.

parameters.name: Lacunarity
parameters.description: How much each octave is scaled. Affects the spacing of detail layers; higher = more contrast between fine and coarse detail.

parameters.name: Gain
parameters.description: How much each octave contributes. Lower gain = smoother; higher = more contrast and sharp variation.

parameters.name: Intensity
parameters.description: Overall strength of the noise (multiplier on the result). Higher = brighter/stronger effect when connected to Output or other nodes.

parameters.name: Time Speed
parameters.description: Speed of time-based animation (for Simplex 3D or animated modes). Higher = faster motion or morphing of the pattern over time.

parameters.name: Time Offset
parameters.description: Offset added to time so you can phase-shift the animation. Change to start the animation at a different point without changing speed.

examples: Connect UV to in, then wire out to Output to see grayscale noise on screen.
Feed Translate or Scale output into in for shifted or zoomed noise; connect Time for animation.
Use noise output with Mix or Smoothstep to drive masks, or with Translate for displacement.
node:noise.advanced: Simplex 2D is fast and smooth for static 2D; Simplex 3D accepts a third coordinate (e.g. time) for animated noise. Value fBm (fractal Brownian motion) stacks octaves for rougher, terrain-like variation.

node:warp-terrain.title: Warp Terrain
node:warp-terrain.tagline: Domain-warped neutral terrain/cloud from 2D value noise FBM with normals and shading (IQ Warp style)
node:warp-terrain.description: Produces an animated organic neutral terrain or cloud pattern using domain-warped 2D value noise FBM with a rotation matrix, finite-difference normals, and simple shading. Based on Inigo Quilez's Warp tutorial. Parameters: Terrain Scale, Time Speed and Time Offset (phase shift the warp without retiming the rest of the graph), Ridge and Bump. Connect UV coordinates to the input; output is vec4 color ready for final output or further effects.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input coordinates (e.g. UV 0–1)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: RGBA color of the warped terrain

node:cubic-curl-noise.title: Curl Noise
node:cubic-curl-noise.tagline: Curl noise magnitude for flow-like, organic patterns
node:cubic-curl-noise.description: Outputs the magnitude of the 2D curl of a 3D potential (cubic value noise with time as z), producing flow-like, divergence-free variation. Parameters: scale, intensity, and time speed/offset. Output is a float in 0–1 range. Use with Final Output or flow-field-pattern for flow effects; for general scalar noise use the Noise node instead.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input UV coordinates to sample the noise

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Curl magnitude 0–1

examples: Connect UV → Curl Noise → Output (or Flow Field Pattern) for flow-like organic patterns.
Drive with Time for animated flow; use with Displace offset for divergence-free displacement.
Combine with Noise or Voronoi for mixed organic and cellular looks.
node:cubic-curl-noise.advanced: Curl of a 3D potential gives divergence-free flow; use when you want smooth, swirling variation. For general scalar noise use the Noise node instead.

node:rings.title: Rings
node:rings.tagline: Perfect for creating concentric ring patterns with customizable spacing and falloff
node:rings.description: Generates concentric ring patterns radiating from a center point. The rings are created by calculating the distance from the center and applying a repeating pattern with configurable frequency and falloff. You can control the center position, ring count (frequency), ring width, and falloff curve to create various effects from sharp concentric circles to soft radial gradients. This is ideal for creating ripple effects, target patterns, or radial organizational structures.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input UV coordinates to generate rings from

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The ring pattern value (typically 0.0 to 1.0 range)

examples: Connect UV or Polar Coords → Rings → Output for concentric circles; tune frequency and falloff.
Use with Domain Repetition for tiled rings; feed into Mix or Smoothstep for masks.
Combine with Noise or Gradient for blended radial effects.
node:rings.advanced: Rings are based on distance from center with repeating phase. Use Polar Coords for best results; falloff softens the ring edges.

node:triangle-grid.title: Triangle Grid
node:triangle-grid.tagline: Regular triangular tiling with configurable edges and edge-to-fill blend
node:triangle-grid.description: Creates a regular triangular grid by placing three families of lines at 0°, 60°, and 120°. Cell sets the size of one triangle in UV space (smaller = denser pattern, larger = fewer / bigger triangles), Line Width sets edge thickness, and Rotation orients the whole pattern. The Edge / Fill mix knob blends from line-only edges (0) to alternating filled triangles (1) — set it anywhere in between for a half-filled look. Intensity scales the final pattern (0 to 2× by default). **Projection:** UV (default) samples the pattern in 2D like most pattern nodes. **Infinite plane** casts a ray from the same fixed camera as the Grid node’s infinite-plane preset, intersects the plane defined by Plane Nx/Ny/Nz and Plane height, and evaluates the triangle pattern in the plane’s tangent space; **Plane tile scale** scales coordinates on that plane (like Grid’s tile scale). Ideal for geometric backgrounds, low-poly style, or tessellation effects when connected to a color map.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input UV coordinates for the grid

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The triangle grid pattern (0–1 by default; up to 2 when Intensity > 1)

examples: Connect UV → Triangle Grid → Output for low-poly or tessellation style; push Edge / Fill mix to 1 for solid alternating triangles.
Use with Domain Repetition or Scale for tiled triangles; mix with Noise for organic edges.
Drop Edge / Fill mix to ~0.3 for a soft half-filled look that reads as both lines and fill at once.
Set Rotation to 30° to flip the dominant triangle orientation; tune Cell and Line Width for sharp edges or soft bands.
Set Projection to Infinite plane and tilt the plane normal for a perspective-like triangle field on screen (same ray setup as Grid’s infinite-plane pattern).
node:triangle-grid.advanced: Three line families at 0°, 60°, 120°; Edge / Fill mix linearly blends the edge mask with the alternating cell parity. Infinite plane uses analytic ray–plane intersection (not iterative raymarching): rays miss or graze the plane yield no contribution. Use for geometric backgrounds or as a mask source.

node:sphere-raymarch.title: Raymarch Sphere
node:sphere-raymarch.tagline: Single-sphere ray march with vector-field distortion; outputs normalized glow (0–1)
node:sphere-raymarch.description: Marches a ray from a fixed viewpoint (origin at z≈3, direction from UV) through a warped distance field built around a sphere radius. A layered vector field distorts the march and drives the look; **Glow**, **Brightness**, and **Steps** control intensity, exposure, and quality. There are no separate position, rotation, lighting, or external camera inputs—use **SDF Raymarch** with Orbit Camera if you need ro/rd-driven 3D scenes. Feed **out** through Output or Mix to color the result.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: UV driving the view ray direction (fixed camera; same pattern as other screen-space 3D helpers)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Normalized glow / intensity in approximately 0–1 (not a raw SDF distance)

examples: Connect UV Coordinates to **in**, then **out** → Output or Mix → Final Output for a quick glowing sphere look.
Raise **Steps** for smoother detail; tune **Field Speed** vs **Time Scale** (both multiply into motion—Field Speed is override-friendly for audio).
For scenes with custom cameras or multiple SDF shapes, use **SDF Raymarch** and wire Orbit Camera **ro**/**rd** plus a graph SDF; this node stays a self-contained single-shape glow.
node:sphere-raymarch.advanced: The march accumulates a cosine-weighted glow along the ray using a sphere-radius field plus vector-field terms (frequencies, harmonics, radial strength). Loop count is capped in shader; the **Steps** parameter clips how many iterations run. As an SDF source for SDF Raymarch, an adapter matches the standalone field (see WGSL/GRM notes).

node:spherical-fibonacci.title: Spherical Fibonacci
node:spherical-fibonacci.tagline: Maps between sphere direction and spherical Fibonacci lattice index for even spot distribution
node:spherical-fibonacci.description: Provides bidirectional mapping between a unit sphere direction (vec3) and a spherical Fibonacci lattice index: direction to index (sf2id), index to direction (id2sf), and the nearest lattice point. Uses the golden-ratio lattice (Keinert et al.) for nearly uniform point distribution on the sphere. Lattice count (N) controls the number of points (4–4096). Connect direction to get index and nearest point; connect index to get the corresponding direction. Reusable primitive for bloom-sphere and other "points on sphere" effects.

inputs.name: direction
inputs.description: Unit direction on the sphere; when unconnected, uses Direction X/Y/Z parameters

inputs.name: index
inputs.description: Lattice index in [0, N-1]; when unconnected, uses Index parameter

outputs.name: index
outputs.description: Nearest lattice index for the input direction (from sf2id)

outputs.name: direction
outputs.description: Sphere direction for the input index (from id2sf)

outputs.name: nearestPoint
outputs.description: Nearest lattice point on the unit sphere to the input direction

node:bloom-sphere.title: Bloom Sphere
node:bloom-sphere.tagline: Sphere with evenly distributed soft spots that pulse in a wave, blue outer glow and reddish inner glow
node:bloom-sphere.description: Renders a sphere covered in soft spots placed on a spherical Fibonacci lattice, with a time-based wave that modulates spot size. Uses analytic ray-sphere intersection and soft projected masks. Outer glow uses the Outer color; inner (facing) areas use the Inner color. Center X/Y shift the sphere in screen space (NDC offset from center). Animation: wave phase (time offset), wave spacing (phase spacing along lattice index), lattice spin around vertical axis, and an optional second sine (detune) for richer motion. Mode: Lattice Glow is the default look; Legacy Bloom matches older graphs. Connect UV to in; output is vec4 for final output or blending. Spot count is capped at 256 for predictable performance (single-pass; no temporal buffer).

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: UV coordinates (e.g. from UV Coords) to raymarch from

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: RGBA color; connect to Final Output or blend with other nodes

examples: Connect UV to in; output goes to Final Output or Mix with a background (e.g. Sky Dome) for a full scene.

Adjust spot count and wave speed for denser or calmer pulse; use wave phase to sync in time, wave spacing for banded vs scrambled shimmer, spin speed to rotate the lattice, and detune for a second temporal frequency.
Use Center X/Y to move the sphere on the canvas without changing UV upstream.
Layer with other vec4 nodes (e.g. Blend Color or Mix) for composite visuals without temporal buffer.
node:bloom-sphere.advanced: Uses the same spherical Fibonacci lattice as the Spherical Fibonacci node for spot placement; analytic ray-sphere intersection keeps it single-pass. For modular reuse of the lattice elsewhere, use Spherical Fibonacci + Bloom Sphere.

node:uv-band-shift.title: Band Shift
node:uv-band-shift.tagline: Primary-axis shear with variable band thickness on the secondary axis
node:uv-band-shift.description: **Orientation** chooses which axis is primary vs secondary. **Horizontal**: bands stack along Y (secondary); each band gets a relative thickness from Secondary min/max (weights are normalized, then **randomly permuted along the axis** so strip order is not tied to generation index—better for glitchy layouts). Primary min/max and Off spr control the X shear offset per band and neighbor smoothing. **Vertical** swaps roles: bands along X, shear along Y. **Bands** is the band count (up to 64). Tight secondary size range and low spreads feel calmer; wide secondary range, more bands, and wider primary offsets feel more chaotic. All in incoming UV space.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input UV or position coordinates

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Shifted coordinates; feed into patterns, noise, or further warps

node:uv-block-glitch.title: Block Glitch
node:uv-block-glitch.tagline: Scattered UV rectangles: position, size, aspect, offset
node:uv-block-glitch.description: Builds up to 16 axis-aligned glitch rectangles in UV space. Each block picks center (CX/CY ranges), half-width W and aspect (height = W / aspect), and a UV delta, all from min/max with **Vary** blending toward the range midpoint for calmer layouts. Later blocks overwrite earlier ones where they overlap. Use narrow center/size ranges and low Vary for subtle tearing; wide ranges, more blocks, and high Vary for chaotic jumps. **Step Hz** (default 0) adds the current time slice index to **Seed** at a fixed rate so the whole layout jumps to a new pattern each step, with no smooth interpolation.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input UV or position coordinates

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Glitch-shifted coordinates

node:volume-rays.title: Cloud Fog
node:volume-rays.tagline: Raymarched volumetric fog/density mask with contrast, falloff, camera, and drift controls
node:volume-rays.description: Marches a ray from the camera through the UV and accumulates layered 3D noise into a fog-like density mask. Use Threshold and Contrast to shape empty vs dense regions, Distance Falloff to keep the volume from washing out, Drift Speed for motion, and Intensity for the final mask strength. Camera position, yaw, pitch, and FOV Scale control the view. Best for abstract fog, nebula-like fields, atmosphere, or layered density masks; use Crepuscular Rays when you need source-based god rays over an image.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input UV coordinates to raymarch from

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Accumulated volumetric density mask (float)

examples: Connect UV → Cloud Fog → Output for abstract fog, nebula, or atmospheric density.
Raise Threshold for more empty space; raise Contrast for sharper pockets of volume.
Combine with Gradient, Mix, or Multiply to layer drifting density over another pattern.
node:volume-rays.advanced: Raymarches through layered 3D noise and applies threshold, contrast, and distance falloff before accumulating density. More steps add depth and smoothness but cost more; smaller step sizes sample the volume more tightly.

node:streak.title: Streak
node:streak.tagline: Directional streak or anamorphic overlay with angle, stretch, and intensity
node:streak.description: Produces a 2D directional streak (anamorphic-style) from the input coordinates. The streak direction is set by angle; width controls the gaussian falloff perpendicular to the streak; stretch controls how far along the streak the falloff extends. Use with mix or multiply to overlay on existing content.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input 2D coordinates (e.g. UV)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Streak intensity (float)

examples: Connect UV → Streak → Mix (with another pattern) for anamorphic or directional streak overlay.
Set angle for streak direction; tune width and stretch for thin or broad streaks.
Use with Output for standalone streak, or Multiply for lens-flare style.
node:streak.advanced: Directional gaussian streak; angle sets direction, width/stretch set shape. Use for anamorphic bokeh or light streaks.

node:shapes-2d.title: Shapes
node:shapes-2d.tagline: Circle (or ellipse) and square (or rounded box) as SDF mask; aspect from size
node:shapes-2d.description: Outputs a 2D shape as a float mask. Shape modes: Circle/Ellipse, Square/Rounded Box, Superellipse, Regular Polygon, Capsule. Size is width and height; unequal width/height stretches shapes. Roundness only affects Square/Rounded Box. Polygon uses Sides; Rotation rotates the shape. Use center to position, softness for edge blur, and intensity to scale the output. Coordinate note: if you feed UV Coords (0–1), set Center to (0.5, 0.5) to place shapes in the middle; if you feed centered coordinates (around 0), leave Center at (0, 0). Ideal for vignettes, panels, and composite shapes with Mix or Final Output.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input 2D coordinates (e.g. UV)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Mask value: 1.0 inside shape, 0.0 outside, soft transition at edge

examples: Connect UV to in; feed output to Output or Mix for circles, ellipses, or rounded squares as masks or panels.
Use unequal width/height for ellipses or rectangles; roundness (Square mode) for soft corners on panels.
Combine with other masks (e.g. Star with Style=Starburst) via Mix or Multiply for composite shapes and vignettes.
node:shapes-2d.advanced: SDF (signed distance function) gives a smooth mask: positive inside, negative outside. Circle vs Square and size control aspect; softness blurs the edge.

node:star-shape-2d.title: Starflower
node:star-shape-2d.tagline: N-point star shape with inner/outer radius, rotation, and softness for masks and patterns.
node:star-shape-2d.description: Outputs a 2D star/flower mask as a float using an SDF. Mode toggles between Star (radial profile) and Starburst (segment-like rays). You control the number of points, inner and outer radius, roundness, rotation, softness, and intensity. Connect UV (or translated coordinates) to the input; use the output with Output or Mix for badges, decorative shapes, or masks.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Position to sample (e.g. UV coordinates)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Star shape value; use with Final Output or Mix for a visible result

parameters.name: Center X
parameters.description: Horizontal center offset. If your input is UV (0–1), 0.5 centers the star; if your input is centered coordinates (around 0), 0.0 centers it.

parameters.name: Center Y
parameters.description: Vertical center offset. If your input is UV (0–1), 0.5 centers the star; if your input is centered coordinates (around 0), 0.0 centers it.

parameters.name: Mode
parameters.description: Choose between Star (radial profile) and Starburst (ray/segment look).

parameters.name: Points
parameters.description: Number of star points (3–32). More points = spikier star; fewer = simpler shape.

parameters.name: Inner Radius
parameters.description: Radius of the inner star points; smaller = pointier star, larger = rounder center.

parameters.name: Outer Radius
parameters.description: Radius of the outer star tips. Larger = bigger star on screen.

parameters.name: Roundness
parameters.description: Softens the inner corners (0 = sharp, 1 = round). Higher = smoother indent between points.

parameters.name: Rotation
parameters.description: Rotation angle in degrees. Change to spin the star/starburst without moving its center.

parameters.name: Softness
parameters.description: Edge softness of the shape. Higher = smoother transition from inside to outside; lower = crisper edge.

parameters.name: Intensity
parameters.description: Scales the output mask value. Higher = stronger mask when connected to Output or Mix.

node:fractal.title: Fractal
node:fractal.tagline: Perfect for generating complex fractal patterns with self-similar structures at multiple scales
node:fractal.description: Generates fractal patterns by iteratively applying transformations and combining results at different scales. Fractals exhibit self-similarity, meaning patterns repeat at different scales, creating complex, organic-looking structures. The node uses iterative algorithms to create these patterns, with configurable iterations, scale factors, and transformation parameters. Fractals are ideal for creating natural-looking patterns, abstract art, or complex geometric structures that would be difficult to create manually.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input UV coordinates to generate the fractal pattern from

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The fractal pattern value

examples: Connect UV to in; feed output to Output for self-similar patterns at multiple scales.
Drive iterations and scale from Time or constants for animated or static fractal detail.
Combine with Noise or Turbulence via Mix for organic, natural-looking backgrounds.
node:fractal.advanced: Fractals repeat the same transformation at different scales; more iterations add detail but cost performance. Use for organic or abstract patterns that are hard to achieve with simple noise.

node:voronoi-noise.title: Voronoi Noise
node:voronoi-noise.tagline: Ideal for creating cellular patterns and organic-looking textures with distinct regions
node:voronoi-noise.description: Generates Voronoi (cellular) noise patterns by dividing space into regions based on proximity to seed points. Each region contains all points closest to a particular seed, creating distinct cellular structures. Output mode: 0 = F1 (distance to nearest seed), 1 = F2−F1 (difference to second-nearest), 2 = edge (smooth edge between cells), 3 = cell ID (normalized hash per cell). Animation: choose Drift (direction and amount), Rotate (UV rotation), or Still. Use Drift Direction (degrees) and Drift Amount for directional flow; Rotation Speed when in Rotate mode.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input UV coordinates to sample the Voronoi pattern

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Voronoi value: F1, F2−F1, edge, or cell ID depending on the selected output mode

examples: Connect UV → Voronoi → Output for cellular patterns; choose F1, F2−F1, edge, or cell ID mode.
Use Drift or Rotate animation for flowing cells; scale controls cell size.
Combine with Noise or Worley via Mix for organic plus cellular texture.
node:voronoi-noise.advanced: Voronoi seeds partition space; F1 is distance to nearest, F2−F1 gives ridges, edge smooths boundaries. Use for cells, scales, or organic regions.

node:worley-noise.title: Worley Noise
node:worley-noise.tagline: Cellular noise with F1, F2−F1, or edge output for organic and crystal-like patterns
node:worley-noise.description: Generates Worley (cellular) noise using the same grid-and-jitter structure as Voronoi. Output mode: F1 (distance to nearest point), F2−F1 (difference to second-nearest), or edge (smooth cell boundaries). Use for organic textures, scales, or abstract cellular patterns. Scale, jitter, and distance metric (Euclidean, Manhattan, Chebyshev) are in the Worley group; output mode and intensity are parameters on this node.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input UV coordinates to sample the Worley pattern

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Worley value: F1, F2−F1, or edge depending on the selected output mode

examples: Connect UV → Worley → Output for cellular or crystal-like patterns; pick F1, F2−F1, or edge.
Tune scale and jitter for cell size and regularity; try Manhattan or Chebyshev for different shapes.
Layer with Voronoi or Noise for mixed organic and cellular looks.
node:worley-noise.advanced: Same grid-and-jitter structure as Voronoi; distance metric (Euclidean, Manhattan, Chebyshev) changes cell shape. Use for scales, crystals, or abstract cells.

node:flow-field-pattern.title: Flow Field
node:flow-field-pattern.tagline: Curl-noise-based flow pattern for organic streaks and flow lines
node:flow-field-pattern.description: Generates a flow-like pattern using 2D curl noise: the curl of a scalar noise field gives a divergence-free vector field, whose magnitude is mapped to a 0–1 float suitable for color mapping. Parameters control spatial scale, curl sampling scale, time speed, octaves, and output intensity. Ideal for organic, fluid-like backgrounds and streak effects.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input UV coordinates to sample the flow field

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Flow pattern value (0–1 range) for color map or mixing

examples: Connect UV → Flow Field → Output for organic flow streaks; tune scale and time speed.
Use with Domain Repetition for tiled flow; combine with Curl Noise for layered flow.
Feed into Mix or Smoothstep for flow-based masks.
node:flow-field-pattern.advanced: Curl-noise magnitude mapped to 0–1; divergence-free so flow lines don't converge. Use for fluid-like or organic streak backgrounds.

node:stripes.title: Stripes
node:stripes.tagline: Directional waveshapes (sine/cos/triangle/square) with rotation, bands, motion, and an optional explicit time drive
node:stripes.description: Rotates UVs and repeats a 1D waveform along that axis — sine/cosine for smooth waves, triangle or square for harder banding — then maps the result into 0–1 for color map or masking. Tune frequency/amplitude/intensity/wave shape, direction, phase, and timing. By default timeline time drives motion; optionally wire Time (or another float) into the dedicated time port.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: UV or other 2D coordinates to sample across the pattern.

inputs.name: time
inputs.description: Seconds (or another driver) multiplied by Time Speed; leave open for built-in wall-clock time.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Pattern value in 0–1 for color mapping, mix, masks, or displacement.

examples: Connect UV → Stripes → Output; switch wave type between sine/square/triangle for soft motion vs hard bands.
Wire Time → Stripes.time for explicit control; tweak phase speed vs time speed.
Layer with Cloud Fog or Flow Field via Mix for fog + banding combos.
node:stripes.advanced: Same engine as the former wave-patterns node: direction rotates the marching axis; frequency and amplitude set band count and contrast; square/triangle approximate older hard-edge stripe looks.

node:dots.title: Dots
node:dots.tagline: Polka-dot grid pattern with configurable spacing, dot size, and falloff
node:dots.description: Generates a seamless grid of dots (polka-dot pattern) by tiling the plane and drawing a circular dot in each cell. Control the spacing between dots, the dot size (radius), and the falloff (softness of the edge). The pattern tiles seamlessly and outputs a float suitable for color map or mix.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input UV coordinates to generate the dot grid from

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The dot pattern value (0.0 to 1.0)

examples: Connect UV → Dots → Output for polka-dot grid; tune spacing and dot size for density.
Use with Domain Repetition or Brick Tiling for offset dot layouts.
Soften falloff for blurred dots or use with Mix for spot patterns.
node:dots.advanced: Seamless grid of circular dots; spacing and radius set layout. Use for fabric, bubbles, or regular spot patterns.

node:gradient.title: Gradient
node:gradient.tagline: Radial or linear gradient (float 0–1) for vignettes, orbs, and linear fades
node:gradient.description: Outputs a single float gradient in 0–1 range. Radial mode: gradient from center with configurable radius and falloff (smoothstep). Linear mode: gradient along an angle with scale. Use with color map, mix, or smoothstep for vignettes, orbs, and directional fades.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input UV coordinates to sample the gradient

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The gradient value (0.0 to 1.0)

examples: Connect UV → Gradient → Output or Mix for radial vignette or linear fade; set radius and falloff.
Use radial for orbs and vignettes; linear for directional fades or sweeps.
Combine with Rings or Distance for blended radial effects.
node:gradient.advanced: Single float 0–1; radial uses distance from center with smoothstep falloff. Linear uses angle and scale for directional gradient.

node:hash32.title: Hash32
node:hash32.tagline: Deterministic vec3 from vec2 for per-cell colors and procedural variation
node:hash32.description: Maps a vec2 (e.g. cell id or UV) to a deterministic vec3 in [0,1] using a Dave Hoskins–style hash (reference: Shadertoy 4djSRW). Useful for disco-style patterns, Voronoi-like cell coloring, and any effect that needs a stable RGB from 2D coordinates.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input 2D coordinates (e.g. cell id or UV)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Deterministic RGB in [0,1]

node:disco-pattern.title: Disco Pattern
node:disco-pattern.tagline: Diamond-grid pattern with per-cell hash color and edge for layered disco-style effects
node:disco-pattern.description: Outputs vec4(RGB, edge): RGB is a deterministic hash color per diagonal cell, alpha is the diamond edge term (0 at edges, 1 at cell centers). Scale and phase parameters let you layer multiple instances with different Scale/Translate for the reference 4-layer multiply look. Connect .rgb (e.g. via Swizzle) to Final Output or Output.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input UV coordinates (e.g. from Scale/Translate)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: RGB = per-cell hash color, A = diamond edge (0–1)

examples: Connect UV → Disco Pattern → Final Output (or Mix) for diamond/cell pattern with per-cell color.
Output RGB is hash color per cell, A is diamond edge; use with Blend Color or Swizzle for variation.
Combine with Voronoi or Stripes for layered disco or retro looks.
node:disco-pattern.advanced: Per-cell hash gives stable color per diamond; alpha gives edge. Use for disco, confetti, or cell-based color without full Voronoi.

node:radial-rays.title: Radial Rays
node:radial-rays.tagline: N rays from a center point with count, spread, width, and falloff
node:radial-rays.description: Generates a radial ray pattern: N evenly spaced rays emanating from a center point. Control ray count, angular spread (full circle or arc), ray width, edge falloff, and rotation. Output is a float suitable for blending or mixing with color maps. Ideal for sunbursts, starbursts, and radial light effects.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input 2D coordinates (e.g. UV)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Ray pattern value (0.0 to 1.0)

examples: Connect UV → Radial Rays → Output or Mix for sunburst or starburst; set count and spread.
Use with Polar Coords for radial layout; tune width and falloff for sharp or soft rays.
Combine with Stripes, Cloud Fog, or Crepuscular Rays for layered light-looking effects.
node:radial-rays.advanced: N rays from a center; spread can be full circle or arc. Use for sunbursts, starbursts, or radial masks.

node:crepuscular-rays.title: Crepuscular Rays
node:crepuscular-rays.tagline: Additive light shafts from a movable source weighted by upstream luminance
node:crepuscular-rays.description: **Post effect**: takes **RGBA color**, derives a procedural ray umbrella in fullscreen UV (`0–1`), multiplies shafts by upstream **luminance**, and adds back tinted light. Controls mirror **Radial Rays**: count/spread/width/intensity plus distance attenuation `(1/(1 + dist·falloff))`. **Rotation speed** (turns/s) × time + offset spins the wedge. **WebGPU** emits a radial sweep pass-plan when routed `… → crepuscular-rays → final-output`; **WebGL** uses inline additive blend on color.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Scene color feeding both mask (luminance) and RGB add

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Input RGB plus additive shafts; alpha forwarded from upstream

examples: Treat like Glow: graded color → **Crepuscular Rays** → **Final Output**; bright regions seed stronger shafts.
Place **Source X/Y** in normalized UV space (+Y up in graph mental model aligns with fullscreen UV conventions).
Animate **rotation speed** for spinning god rays; lower **spread** gives sun-through-slit beams.
node:crepuscular-rays.advanced: Downstream compilers differ: fullscreen WebGPU may promote this to GPU sweep passes; previews keep single-pass additive modulation.

node:gradient-mask.title: Gradient Mask
node:gradient-mask.tagline: Ideal for creating smooth gradient masks for selective effect application and blending
node:gradient-mask.description: Creates gradient masks that can selectively apply or blend effects in specific regions. The node supports multiple mask types: radial (circular gradients from a center point), linear (gradients along a direction), and elliptical (oval-shaped gradients). Each mask type has configurable position, size, falloff, and strength parameters. The mask outputs a value from 0.0 (fully masked) to 1.0 (fully visible), allowing smooth transitions. This is essential for creating vignettes, spotlight effects, or any region-based effect application.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input color to apply the gradient mask to

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The masked color output with gradient blending applied

node:hexagonal-grid.title: Hex Grid
node:hexagonal-grid.tagline: Perfect for creating hexagonal grid patterns for tiling, cellular structures, or game board layouts
node:hexagonal-grid.description: Generates a seamless, regular hexagonal tiling pattern. Control the cell size, the gap (margin) between cells, and a per-cell rotation (rotates each hexagon’s shape without rotating the grid). A separate grid rotation rotates the overall tiling, and intensity controls the output strength.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input UV coordinates to generate the hexagonal grid from

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The hexagonal grid pattern value

examples: Connect UV → Hex Grid → Output for honeycomb or tiled hexagons; tune cell size and gap.
Use with Domain Repetition or Scale for more tiles; per-cell rotation for twisted hexes.
Combine with Noise or Rings via Mix for organic or radial hex patterns.
node:hexagonal-grid.advanced: Seamless hex tiling; gap sets margin between cells. Grid rotation and per-cell rotation give layout variety. Use for game boards, cells, or tessellation.

node:hex-prism-sdf.title: Hex Prism SDF
node:hex-prism-sdf.description: Signed distance to a hex prism. When out feeds SDF Raymarch sdf, the march evaluates distance along each ray—you usually leave Position unwired. For direct use, wire vec3 Position. Hex radius sets cross-section size; half height sets half the extrusion length along Z before Pos offset. Float out suits SDF Raymarch, Repeated Hex Prism SDF, or effects downstream.

inputs.name: position
inputs.description: 3D sample point. Unwired with SDF Raymarch sdf: the march supplies p. Wire for standalone sampling or Constant Vec3 experiments.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Signed distance to the hex prism surface

examples: Connect out → SDF Raymarch sdf; Orbit Camera ro/rd and UV → raymarcher for a single prism—leave Position unwired.
Camera ro/rd and UV → SDF Raymarch; Hex Prism SDF → sdf → Output.
Tune hex radius and half height for profile; chain into Repeated Hex Prism SDF for an infinite prism grid.
node:hex-prism-sdf.advanced: Extrusion axis is Z in shape space before Pos offset. Raymarch uses world-space steps. For seamless tiling prefer Repeated Hex Prism SDF.

node:repeated-hex-prism-sdf.title: Repeated Hex Prism SDF
node:repeated-hex-prism-sdf.tagline: 3D SDF: infinite grid of hex prisms for raymarching
node:repeated-hex-prism-sdf.description: Infinite lattice of hex prisms as a float **SDF**. When **out** feeds **SDF Raymarch**’s **`sdf`** port, each march step evaluates distance at that step’s ray sample—the marcher injects sample **Position** for you (`p` → this node); you do **not** run a separate wire into **Position** (the setup-example graph wires only sdf). For offline tests or graphs without SDF Raymarch, wire **Position** yourself (e.g. **Constant Vec3**). Cells repeat per axis via `mod` with offsets from **Spacing X/Y/Z**, then standard hex prism distance; tweak **Hex radius** and **Half height** plus spacing. Classic touchstone: Shadertoy hex-prism raymarch.

inputs.name: position
inputs.description: 3D sample point. With **SDF Raymarch sdf** wired from this node’s **out**, leave unwired—the march passes each step’s position automatically. Otherwise wire explicitly (Constant Vec3, etc.).

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Signed distance to the repeated hex prism grid

examples: UV → Orbit Camera; **Repeated Hex Prism SDF** **out** → **SDF Raymarch** **sdf** (screen UV + camera ro/rd into the marcher); **Position** stays unwired—then **Output** → **Final Output**.

Tune **Spacing** vs **Hex radius**: too-large radius overlaps neighboring cells or swallows the lattice; asymmetric **Spacing X/Y/Z** is axis-aligned repetition, not a strict 2D honeycomb tiling in XY.
Animate or audio-drive **spacing**/**hex radius** (see preset sdf-raymarcher-hex-audio) against **SDF Raymarch** glow or color passes.
node:repeated-hex-prism-sdf.advanced: Same prism orientation family as **Hex Prism SDF** (extrusion along Z inside each repeated cell before any manual Pos offsets). Unequal spacing stretches the lattice on each axis independently. Avoid parameters where **Hex radius** is a large fraction of the relevant cross-section spacing—distance fields get harsh and marching can behave poorly at cell seams.

node:particle-system.title: Speckle Grain
node:particle-system.tagline: Perfect for creating animated particle effects with configurable movement, size, and behavior
node:particle-system.description: Generates animated particle systems that create dynamic, moving particle effects. Each particle has position, velocity, size, and lifetime properties that evolve over time. The particles can be rendered as points, circles, or other shapes, and can interact with forces, boundaries, or other particles. The system supports configurable particle count, spawn rate, initial velocity, gravity, and various behaviors. This is ideal for creating effects like sparks, smoke, rain, or any collection of moving elements.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input UV coordinates to render particles at

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The particle system output (particle presence or density)

examples: Connect UV → Speckle Grain → Output for animated particles; drive with Time for motion.
Tune count, size, and behavior for sparks, rain, or abstract motion.
Combine with Distance or other patterns for particle density or masking.
node:particle-system.advanced: Particles have position, velocity, size, lifetime; configurable spawn and forces. Use for sparks, smoke, rain, or any moving point set.

node:plane-grid.title: Grid
node:plane-grid.tagline: Ideal for creating regular square grid patterns for tiling, organization, or structural elements
node:plane-grid.description: Generates regular square grid patterns by dividing space into uniform cells. The grid creates a checkerboard or lattice-like pattern that can be used for tiling, organization, or as a structural element in compositions. You can control the grid cell size, line thickness, and offset. The grid can output cell IDs, distance to grid lines, or edge detection. This is useful for creating game boards, measurement grids, or any application requiring regular spatial division.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input UV coordinates to generate the grid from

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The grid pattern value

node:box-torus-sdf.title: Primitives
node:box-torus-sdf.tagline: 3D SDF primitives and raymarching — listed in the palette as Primitives (node id box-torus-sdf)
node:box-torus-sdf.description: Renders 3D primitives using signed distance fields (SDF) with raymarching. In the palette this node is named Primitives; searches and docs may refer to it as box-torus-sdf. Shape types: 0=Box, 1=Torus, 2=Capsule, 3=Cylinder (capped; radius X, half-height Y), 4=Cone (height Y, base radius X, top radius Z), 5=Round cone (base X, tip radius Z, height Y), 6=Octahedron (scale X), 7=Icosahedron (scale X). All share Position (X/Y/Z), Size, Rotation, and Appearance (glow strength, raymarch steps). Output is lit grayscale shading (depth factor plus optional glow term, multiplied by lighting), not a raw signed distance. Where the ray misses geometry the output stays at zero; the glow term is evaluated only on a surface hit. Lighting supports directional or point light with optional shadows. Specular group: enable Cook-Torrance (GGX + Fresnel) for PBR-style specular; Roughness and F0 control the look. Optional Ray origin and Ray direction: when connected (e.g. from Orbit Camera), the node uses that camera; when unconnected, Ro X/Y/Z under Camera set the built-in ray origin and UV drives the default perspective ray direction.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input UV coordinates to raymarch from

inputs.name: ro
inputs.description: Ray origin (camera position); when unconnected, uses Ro X/Y/Z parameters

inputs.name: rd
inputs.description: Ray direction; when unconnected, built from UV for perspective

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Lit grayscale shading: surface lighting (and optional Cook-Torrance specular) combined with a depth-based factor plus the Glow parameter’s contribution along the ray. Zero when the ray misses the shape.

examples: Connect Look-at Camera or Orbit Camera ro/rd to ro/rd inputs; connect UV to in, then output to Output or Final Output.
Without Orbit Camera, open the Camera section and adjust Ro X/Y/Z to dolly the built-in view.
Switch shape type (Box, Torus, Capsule, Cylinder, Cone, Octahedron, Icosahedron) for different primitives; use Size and Rotation to pose.
Enable Cook-Torrance in Specular for PBR-style reflections; add directional or point light and optional shadows.
node:box-torus-sdf.advanced: When Ray origin / Ray direction inputs are unconnected, Ro X/Y/Z (Camera section) set the built-in ray origin and UV builds a perspective ray direction. For a shared rig across multiple 3D nodes, connect one Orbit Camera or Look-at Camera to all. SDF combination order (if you later combine with other SDFs) affects which shape is in front.

node:glass-shell.title: Glass Shell
node:glass-shell.tagline: Refractive glass shell: outer SDF → refract → inner SDF → shade; object inside glass effect
node:glass-shell.description: Two-stage raymarch: march outer SDF (sphere, box, or icosahedron), at hit refract the ray with IOR, then march inner SDF (sphere, box, or smooth sphere-plus-box blend). Shades the inner hit with diffuse and optional microfacet specular (Cook-Torrance), or uses the background OKLCH color (converted to linear RGB in-shader) if the refracted ray misses the inner shell. Inner albedo uses the same OKLCH picker + L/C/H sliders as Skydome. Adds outer highlights on the glass. Output is RGBA vec4—alpha is 0 where the outer shell is missed (transparent for blending) and 1 on hits. Optional ro/rd inputs: connect Orbit Camera for a shared rig; when ro is unwired, use Fallback ray origin (Ray origin X/Y/Z parameters). Smooth blend (inner) applies only when inner shape is Sphere + box (smooth).

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: UV coordinates (e.g. from UV Coords) to raymarch from

inputs.name: ro
inputs.description: Ray origin (camera position); when unconnected, uses Ray origin X/Y/Z under Fallback ray origin

inputs.name: rd
inputs.description: Ray direction; when unconnected, built from UV for perspective

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: RGBA color; connect to Final Output or blend with other nodes

examples: Connect UV Coordinates to in and Orbit Camera ro/rd for a draggable view; use the color pickers for Inner and Background OKLCH, then plug out into Blend or Final Output.
If refracted rays miss the inner surface, shading uses the Background color—you will still see outer glass highlights.
Set inner shape to Sphere + box (smooth) to reveal Smooth blend (inner); other inner shapes hide that knob because it does nothing.
Turn off Physics for a simpler Phong-style highlight on inner and outer surfaces.

node:radial-repeat-sdf.title: Radial Repeat SDF
node:radial-repeat-sdf.tagline: 3D SDF primitive: concentric spherical shells for Shadertoy-style raymarching
node:radial-repeat-sdf.description: Radial shells in 3D SDF space—concentric spheres you can feed into **SDF Raymarch** or mix with other distances. **Shell spacing** is the radial distance between repeats (same role as the old **Period** control). **Ring phase** is **0–1** inside each spacing: the radial offset to the shell peak is **phase × spacing** (so phase **0.5** with spacing **1** matches a half-spacing offset). Legacy graphs with **Period** / **Half period** migrate to **Shell spacing** / **Ring phase** (phase = half period ÷ period, clamped to 0–1). Formula: abs(mod(length(p), shellSpacing) - ringPhase × shellSpacing). When **SDF** (this node’s float out) drives **SDF Raymarch** **sdf**, the raymarcher evaluates this node at each march step and supplies **Position** for you—no extra **Position** wire unless you want a manual offset or use this node outside that path.

inputs.name: position
inputs.description: 3D sample point. When this node’s **SDF** out is wired to **SDF Raymarch** **sdf**, the march step position is supplied automatically. Connect **Position** yourself for standalone graphs, constants, or explicit offsets.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Signed distance to the concentric shells

node:kifs-sdf.title: KIFS SDF
node:kifs-sdf.tagline: Fold–scale–offset–rotate IFS distance for Larval-style raymarching
node:kifs-sdf.description: Signed distance from a 3D Kaleidoscopic IFS: absolute fold, then scale, offset, and rotate each iteration. Wire out → SDF Raymarch sdf; SDF Raymarch supplies Position at each march step—add Orbit Camera ro and rd for Larval-style camera motion. Rotation axis X/Y/Z is normalized in-shader; angle is radians on the sliders. Minimum scale is 1 so each iteration expands the folded domain like usual KIFS; Core radius trims the iterated point with an inner sphere. Distance is approximate like other iterated folds—increase raymarch steps or tighten safety on SDF Raymarch if surfaces band or leak.

inputs.name: position
inputs.description: 3D sample position, typically from SDF Raymarch when this node feeds sdf

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Signed distance estimate to the KIFS surface

node:mandelbox-sdf.title: Mandelbox SDF
node:mandelbox-sdf.tagline: Classic Mandelbox fold–scale–translate distance for raymarching
node:mandelbox-sdf.description: Signed distance to a Mandelbox-style fractal: box fold, sphere fold, then scale and offset each iteration. Conservative DE with a tunable DE multiplier (deFudge); values below 1 can overstep and punch through surfaces—defaults are slightly conservative. Distance fields from iterated folds are estimates: lower raymarch step counts or raise DE multiplier if banding or leaks appear. Folding limit drives both box extent (±limit) and sphere-fold strength; min radius floors the sphere fold. Try ~64–256 raymarch steps for detail; combine with Displacement 3D or Repeated Hex Prism SDF for layered scenes.

inputs.name: position
inputs.description: 3D sample position (from SDF Raymarch when connected as SDF)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Signed distance estimate to the Mandelbox surface

node:menger-sponge-sdf.title: Menger Sponge SDF
node:menger-sponge-sdf.tagline: Classic Menger-style 3D fractal SDF for the SDF Raymarch
node:menger-sponge-sdf.description: Signed distance to a Menger sponge–style fractal: recursive **3×3×3 void cuts** in a cube. Wire **out** → **SDF Raymarch** **sdf**; wire **Position** from the raymarcher’s sample positions or use **Fallback pos** sliders when it is unwired. **Rot axis X/Y/Z** choose the rotation axis together — the shader normalizes; **Angle rad** is radians. **DE scale** multiplies distance into the marcher: higher enlarges ray steps for speed at more risk through holes; lower is tighter and safer on thin sponge. Float parameters support timeline automation and audio bands; iterations 1–5 are fixed at compile time like similar fractal nodes.

inputs.name: position
inputs.description: 3D sample point from the raymarcher **Position** connection when wired; otherwise **Fallback pos** X/Y/Z.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Signed distance to the sponge surface

node:sierpinski-tetra-sdf.title: Sierpinski Tetra SDF
node:sierpinski-tetra-sdf.tagline: Tetrahedral IFS SDF with triangular lacunarity for the SDF Raymarch
node:sierpinski-tetra-sdf.description: Signed distance field for a **Sierpinski tetrahedron–style** IFS: **tetrahedral symmetry folds**, Knighty / Klems wedge, then **IFS scale** and **offset** each iteration. **IFS scale** is the per-iteration contraction factor, not an overall zoom. Optional **axis–angle pre-rotation**: axis is normalized in the shader; **Angle rad** is radians. Inner primitive is a **scaled sphere** in iterated space, KIFS-style, **not** a full `sdTetrahedron` shell—tune **Core radius** and **DE bias** under Advanced. Higher **Iterations** increases raymarch cost with the SDF Raymarch. Connect **out** to the SDF Raymarch **sdf** port. **Float** parameters support timeline automation and audio where marked; **Iterations** is a capped int, no audio, same policy as KIFS / Menger.

inputs.name: position
inputs.description: 3D sample position. When wired, use the raymarcher sample; when unwired, **Pos X**, **Pos Y**, **Pos Z** under Manual position.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Signed distance to the fractal surface; sphere-core estimate.

node:julia-slab-sdf.title: Julia Slab SDF
node:julia-slab-sdf.tagline: 2D Julia escape field in a thin 3D slab (not Mandelbulb topology)
node:julia-slab-sdf.description: Outputs a signed distance field from a **2D quadratic Julia** iteration **z ← z² + c** on **XY** (scaled), using an **exterior distance estimate** when |z| exceeds the escape radius: **0.5·log(max(|z|,ε))·|z| / |dz/dz0|**, then combines with a **slab** around **z = 0** via **max(|z₃| − halfThickness, d_fractal)** so the visible surface is a thin band with fractal boundary detail. **Not** a 3D Mandelbulb or orbital Julia in vec4. Connect **position** into the **SDF Raymarch** chain and wire **out** → **sdf**. Float parameters (**Julia c.x / c.y**, **XY scale**, **slab half-thickness**, **escape radius**) support timeline automation and audio where marked; **max iterations** is an int (no audio) to keep preview cost predictable—raise it only when needed.

inputs.name: position
inputs.description: 3D position to evaluate the SDF at (e.g. raymarch sample point)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Signed distance (slab ∩ 2D Julia exterior estimate in XY)

node:mandelbulb-sdf.title: Mandelbulb SDF
node:mandelbulb-sdf.tagline: Classic Mandelbulb distance field for **SDF Raymarch**
node:mandelbulb-sdf.description: Triplex Mandelbulb iteration with a clamped distance-estimation step. Wire **out** to **sdf** on **SDF Raymarch**. **Power** is an integer **2–24** with no audio or animation—it shapes the fractal without stepped automation pops; values above ~12 often need more **Steps** on **SDF Raymarch** alongside higher **Iterations**. **Bailout** and **DE fudge** under **Advanced DE** rescale the estimate—fast automation there often flickers. **Sphere blend** mixes DE with a simple outer sphere cap for stability at some cost to fidelity.

inputs.name: position
inputs.description: Sample position on the raymarch SDF chain, usually the marching evaluation point

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Distance estimate toward the Mandelbulb surface

node:mandelbulb-sdf.advanced: Strong warning: fast automation or audio on **Bailout** or **DE fudge** tends to flicker because the distance field is not tame under those moves. Prefer slow sweeps or small ranges. If the preview gets noisy, lower **Iterations** first, then raise **Steps** on **SDF Raymarch**. **Sphere blend** is the stability lever when the DE field gets harsh.

node:ether-sdf.title: Ether SDF
node:ether-sdf.tagline: 3D SDF for Ether-fork style volumetric blobs: time-rotated domain and layered sine
node:ether-sdf.description: Outputs a signed distance field (SDF) for the Ether-fork style effect: time-based 3D rotations (XZ and XY planes) and a composite formula (radial term plus layered sine). Connect to SDF Raymarch as the SDF input. Parameters: Rotate XZ/XY, Time Speed/Offset, Scale, Wobble Speed, Sine Amp; Pos X/Y/Z (center); Breathe amt/speed (Z-only bob). Set Time Speed to 0 to freeze. Reference: Shadertoy ltfXzj (Ether by nimitz).
node:ether-sdf.advanced: Very high Scale, Sine Amp, or Wobble Speed—or fast automation/audio on them—can make the march noisier; ease values or raise **Steps** on **SDF Raymarch**.

inputs.name: position
inputs.description: 3D position to evaluate the SDF at (e.g. raymarch sample point)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Signed distance (Ether-style volumetric blob field)

node:displacement-3d.title: Displace 3D
node:displacement-3d.tagline: Signed 3D value-noise FBM displacement for raymarching and SDF distortion
node:displacement-3d.description: Computes a vec3 displacement from 3D position (and time) using signed value-noise FBM. Input: vec3 position. Output: vec3 displacement to add to position (e.g. position + displacement before SDF evaluation in a raymarcher). Parameters: scale, octaves, lacunarity, gain, amplitude; time speed and offset for animation. Uses three offset samples for uncorrelated x,y,z components.

inputs.name: position
inputs.description: 3D position to sample displacement at (e.g. raymarch sample point)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Displacement vector to add to position

node:generic-raymarcher.title: SDF Raymarch
node:generic-raymarcher.tagline: Graph SDF raymarch: **Glow** reads stylized along the ray; **Color** is hit tint with fog. Optional camera and displacement.
node:generic-raymarcher.description: Marches rays through an SDF you connect to **sdf** — float distance from nodes with a **Position** input, e.g. Repeated Hex Prism SDF, Radial Repeat SDF, Ether SDF. The march loop passes the current sample **Position** into that subgraph each step, so you usually wire only **sdf** from the SDF primitive. Feed **Screen position**, often UV Coords via Orbit Camera, so each pixel gets its own ray. **Glow** is an emissive-style scalar along the march, not lit PBR; use **Color** or downstream grading for readable surface color. Optionally wire **Displacement** and camera **ro**/**rd**. On **WebGPU** (MVP preview), if **sdf** is unwired or the connected SDF node is not in the supported allow-list, **Glow** and **Color** are zero (black)—there is no implicit fallback distance.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Screen position per pixel, e.g. UV Coords; feeds the default ray direction when **rd** is unconnected.

inputs.name: sdf
inputs.description: Graph SDF distance; evaluated each march step at the displaced sample position.

inputs.name: displacement
inputs.description: Optional displacement added to sample position before SDF

inputs.name: ro
inputs.description: Ray origin, e.g. from Orbit Camera; when unconnected, **Origin X/Y/Z** apply.

inputs.name: rd
inputs.description: Unit ray direction, e.g. from Orbit Camera; when unconnected, derived from **Screen position** as forward rays with z = -1.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Stylized **Glow** along the ray — ethereal or volumetric modulation, not a classic depth buffer.

outputs.name: color
outputs.description: Hit color with depth fog: position-based RGB minus step count; zero on miss.

parameters.name: Steps
parameters.description: March iterations — trades quality against cost. Higher = tighter captures / fewer halos; clamped between 16 and 200 in the shader. Not automatable: use the slider so preview cost stays predictable.

parameters.name: Glow
parameters.description: Scales the emissive-style scalar along the ray. Often pairs with **Output**, **Brightness**, or **Mix**.

parameters.name: Brightness
parameters.description: Exposure multiplier stacked with Glow for the scalar output.

parameters.name: Origin X
parameters.description: Fallback ray origin X when **ro** is unplugged.

parameters.name: Origin Y
parameters.description: Fallback ray origin Y when **ro** is unplugged.

parameters.name: Origin Z
parameters.description: Fallback ray origin Z when **ro** is unplugged.

node:generic-raymarcher.advanced: Upstream SDF/displacement subgraphs obey the usual parameter precedence: timeline automation when armed, then parameter-port inputs respecting each input mode, then fixed slider/uniform defaults.

node:iridescent-tunnel.title: Iridescent Tunnel
node:iridescent-tunnel.tagline: Volumetric raymarched infinite cylinders in 3D repeated, warped space with view-dependent iridescent coloring
node:iridescent-tunnel.description: Renders an infinite tunnel of repeating cylinders in 3D space with warp, Z-rotation, and soft volumetric accumulation. Center X/Y use the same space as your UV input: with UV Coordinates (symmetric screen space around the middle), defaults keep the focal point centered; lower min / raise max on the sliders to pan farther in any direction, including farther past the corners. View-dependent iridescent coloring mixes two OKLCH-picked colors based on viewing angle (Color A / Color B). Parameters: center, colors (OKLCH), repetition scale (cell size), tube radius, warp frequency and strength, camera speed, rotation, zoom (field of view), raymarch steps, density, iridescence mix and color shift. Connect UV to in; output is vec4 RGBA for blending with Mix or Blend mode. Wiring straight into Final Output uses RGB only (alpha is dropped there), matching typical fullscreen output—use Blend/Mix nodes if you need the stored alpha.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: UV coordinates to raymarch from (e.g. from UV Coords)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: RGBA color; connect to Final Output for fullscreen (RGB only; alpha discarded) or to Mix / Blend mode to combine with other layers

node:inflated-icosahedron.title: Icosahedron
node:inflated-icosahedron.tagline: Raymarched 3D inflated icosahedron with domain folding, twist, per-face palette, soft shadows and lighting
node:inflated-icosahedron.description: Renders a raymarched 3D inflated icosahedron with icosahedron domain folding (knighty), twist around the nearest vertex (tdhooper), optional 3D polar repetition for seamless looping, per-face IQ-style palette coloring, soft shadows, ambient occlusion, and lighting. **Look:** Palette hue shifts the face rainbow; shape size scales the SDF; light angle spins the key light around the vertical axis; contrast adjusts shaded midtones. **Background** (collapsible): inner/outer OKLCH color pickers define the screen-space gradient and gradient radius sets how far from center the blend reaches. Connect Screen position (e.g. UV Coords) to in; optionally connect camera ro/rd. Output is vec3 RGB.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Screen position for ray direction (e.g. UV Coords)

inputs.name: ro
inputs.description: Ray origin (camera position); when unconnected uses built-in orbit

inputs.name: rd
inputs.description: Ray direction; when unconnected derived from in and ro

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Final RGB color; connect to Final Output or color nodes

node:iterated-inversion.title: Spotlight
node:iterated-inversion.tagline: Orbiting circle folds with twist—stack soft tinted blobs into flowing organic color
node:iterated-inversion.description: Runs an internal loop of circle inversions around an orbiting center with angle twist, accumulating gaussian blobs into a vec3 color. Based on the "Inversion 2" effect (Inigo Quilez, Shadertoy 4t3SzN). Uses aspect-correct UV so circles stay round. Parameters: iterations, time speed, twist, orbit radius, scale (zoom); appearance: blob strength, sharpness, exposure; color: hue offset, hue spread, hue angle. Connect UV to in; output to Final Output or Color Grading.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: Input UV coordinates (e.g. from UV Coords)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Accumulated color from iterated inversions

node:metaballs.title: Metaballs
node:metaballs.tagline: 3D raymarched metaballs: soft blobs that blend where their fields overlap
node:metaballs.description: Renders 3D metaballs using raymarching. Metaballs are soft blobs whose influence fields are summed; the visible surface is where the combined field exceeds a threshold. Blob count (2–6), radius, threshold, and orbit radius control the shape; blobs orbit around the scene center. Use Position to move the group, Animation for orbit speed, and Appearance for raymarch steps and glow. Connect UV to in and wire out to Final Output or Mix for shading.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: UV coordinates to raymarch from (e.g. from UV Coords)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Surface and glow value (float); use with Final Output or Mix

examples: Connect UV to in; output to Output or Mix for 3D metaballs (soft blobs that merge where fields overlap).
Use with Orbit Camera or built-in orbit; adjust blob count, radius, threshold, and orbit radius for shape and motion.
Feed output to Output or Mix for shading; layer with Primitives or Raymarch Sphere for mixed 3D scenes.
node:metaballs.advanced: Metaball surface is where the sum of blob influence fields exceeds a threshold; blobs orbit the scene center. More blobs (2–6) and higher raymarch steps improve quality at higher cost.

node:sky-dome.title: Skydome
node:sky-dome.tagline: Procedural sky dome with zenith/horizon gradient, optional sun disc, and a virtual camera (FOV, yaw, pitch, roll)
node:sky-dome.description: Outputs a procedural sky as vec4 (RGBA) suitable for use behind raymarched 3D scenes. Sky color is blended from horizon to zenith; an optional sun disc is added when the view ray is close to the sun direction. Use Sky group to set zenith and horizon as OKLCH colors (click the swatches for the picker, or edit L/C/H directly) and horizon softness; Sun group sets direction (Dir X/Y/Z), radius (radians), and intensity; View group sets the virtual camera — FOV (degrees), yaw/pitch (XY pad: pan and tilt), and roll. Connect UV (e.g. from UV Coords) to in; connect out to mix, final output, or Blend Channel / Blend Color.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: UV coordinates to compute sky ray direction (e.g. from UV Coords)

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: Sky color (RGB) and alpha 1.0

examples: Connect UV to in; output to Mix or Final Output as background behind raymarched 3D (e.g. Primitives, Raymarch Sphere, SDF Raymarch).
Click the Sky swatches to open the OKLCH color picker (or tweak L/C/H knobs directly); add a sun disc with Sun direction and radius for a full sky look.

Use the View group to frame the sky: FOV widens or narrows the dome, the Yaw/Pitch pad pans and tilts the virtual camera, and Roll cants the horizon — useful for matching the camera of a 3D scene mixed underneath.
Use as the second input to Mix with 3D node output (e.g. box-torus-sdf) so the sky shows where the ray misses geometry.
node:sky-dome.advanced: Sky ray direction is derived from UV with an adjustable virtual camera. The default FOV of 90° reproduces the legacy fixed projection (uv*2-1, -1.0); shrinking FOV zooms in, widening it shows more of the dome. Yaw rotates around vertical, pitch tilts around horizontal, and roll rotates around the forward axis (applied as roll → pitch → yaw). Horizon softness widens the horizon→zenith blend band. Sun disc is drawn when the view ray is close to the sun direction; intensity and radius (radians) control brightness and size. Colors are stored in OKLCH for perceptually uniform editing and tween better than raw RGB.

node:block-displacement.title: Block Displacement
node:block-displacement.tagline: Perfect for creating glitch effects with variable-size blocks that displace coordinates
node:block-displacement.description: Creates glitch effects by dividing the coordinate space into variable-size blocks and applying displacement offsets. Larger blocks receive less offset while smaller blocks receive more offset, creating a distinctive glitch aesthetic. The blocks can be oriented horizontally or vertically, and the block boundaries, sizes, and offsets are determined by configurable parameters including block count, spacing chaos, and time-based animation. This effect is commonly used with block-color-glitch and block-edge-brightness nodes to create cohesive glitch compositions.

inputs.name: in
inputs.description: The input 2D coordinates to apply block displacement to

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The displaced coordinates after block transformation

node:compare.title: Compare
node:compare.tagline: Perfect for comparing two values and outputting binary results for conditional logic
node:compare.description: Compares two input values using a selected comparison operation and outputs 1.0 if the condition is true, 0.0 otherwise. The available operations include equal (==), not equal (!=), less than (<), less than or equal (<=), greater than (>), and greater than or equal (>=). This is essential for creating conditional logic, thresholds, and binary masks based on value comparisons. The output can be used to drive other nodes or create selective effects.

inputs.name: a
inputs.description: The first value to compare

inputs.name: b
inputs.description: The second value to compare against

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: 1.0 if the comparison is true, 0.0 otherwise

examples: Compare Noise or Distance to a threshold (e.g. equal, greater) for binary masks.
Use output as condition for Select to pick one of two values.
Drive Mix factor or gradient mask from comparison (1.0 or 0.0).
Feed Mask BW or Mask Color mask inputs for sharp region splits (1.0 or 0.0).
node:compare.advanced: Output is 1.0 when condition true, 0.0 otherwise. For smooth transitions use Smoothstep; for hard edges use Step.

node:select.title: Select
node:select.tagline: Ideal for choosing between two values based on a condition for conditional routing
node:select.description: Selects between two input values based on a condition. If the condition is greater than 0.5, it returns the true value; otherwise, it returns the false value. Port order is If true, If false, then Condition (bottom). When the condition port is unwired, the Condition control on the node supplies the value—typically 0.0–1.0, where values above 0.5 count as true. The If true and If false branches are wired-only (no duplicate body controls). Useful for conditional routing, switching inputs, or if-else style logic.

inputs.name: trueValue
inputs.description: The value returned when condition is true

inputs.name: falseValue
inputs.description: The value returned when condition is false

inputs.name: condition
inputs.description: The condition value (values > 0.5 select trueValue). Unwired: use the Condition control on the node.

outputs.name: out
outputs.description: The selected value based on the condition

examples: Wire Compare or Step into Condition for hard branching between two floats or colors.
Choose between two Output outputs for a simple A/B look.
Use panel If true / If false when ports are unwired; connect If true / If false to override with graph values.
node:select.advanced: Condition treats values above 0.5 as true. For grayscale masks near 0.5, small changes can flip the branch—use Smoothstep or clamp if you need stability.

node:mask-composite-float.title: Mask BW
node:mask-composite-float.tagline: Composites foreground over background using a float mask; dark areas show background, bright areas show foreground.
